meta.title: Open MCAT Practice Questions
meta.note: Original MCAT-style practice questions dedicated to the public domain (CC0 1.0). Free for anyone to use for any purpose, including commercial use and redistribution, with no attribution required. NOT affiliated with or endorsed by the AAMC; contains NO real AAMC exam items — every question is original writing. Generated, then screened by an independent blind re-solve audit that removed mis-keyed or ambiguous items.
meta.verified: Every item was independently re-solved blind by a second panel; items the panel disagreed with or flagged were removed.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following amino acids is achiral (lacks a stereocenter at the alpha carbon)?
questions.explanation: Glycine's side chain is a single hydrogen, so its alpha carbon bears two identical H atoms and is not a stereocenter, making glycine the only achiral standard amino acid.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A neutral amino acid has pKa values of 2.3 (alpha-carboxyl) and 9.7 (alpha-amino). At physiological pH (7.4), which species predominates?
options: The fully protonated form, net charge +1
The fully deprotonated form, net charge -1
An equal mixture of the +1 and -1 species
questions.explanation: At a pH between the two pKa values, the carboxyl is deprotonated (COO-) and the amino group is protonated (NH3+), giving the zwitterion with a net charge of zero.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Glutamate has pKa values of about 2.2 (alpha-COOH), 4.3 (side-chain COOH), and 9.7 (alpha-NH3+). Its isoelectric point (pI) is closest to:
questions.explanation: The pI is the average of the two pKa values flanking the neutral species. For an acidic amino acid these are the two lowest pKa values: (2.2 + 4.3)/2 is approximately 3.3.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which amino acid side chain has a pKa nearest physiological pH, making it an effective buffer and a common general acid/base catalyst in enzyme active sites?
questions.explanation: The imidazole side chain of histidine has a pKa near 6, close to physiological pH, so it can readily gain or lose a proton and often serves as a catalytic residue.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The peptide bond shows restricted rotation and planarity. The best explanation is that the bond:
options: Has partial double-bond character from resonance, restricting rotation
Is a full carbon-carbon double bond
Involves a covalent bond from the carbonyl oxygen to the next nitrogen
Is locked in place only by bulky side chains
questions.explanation: Resonance delocalizes the amide nitrogen lone pair into the carbonyl, giving the C-N bond partial double-bond character; this restricts rotation and holds the six peptide-bond atoms in a plane.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Alpha helices and beta pleated sheets are stabilized primarily by:
options: Ionic bonds between charged side chains
Hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl (C=O) and amide (N-H) groups
Hydrophobic packing of the protein core
questions.explanation: Secondary structure is defined by regular hydrogen bonding along the polypeptide backbone (C=O to N-H), independent of the identity of the side chains.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Heating a globular protein until it denatures disrupts higher-order folding but leaves which level of structure intact?
questions.explanation: Denaturation disrupts noncovalent interactions and can break disulfides, but it does not hydrolyze the covalent peptide bonds, so the primary sequence remains intact.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Proline is rarely found within the interior of an alpha helix. The best explanation is that proline:
options: Is positively charged and repels the helix dipole
Has a rigid ring and its nitrogen lacks an amide hydrogen needed for helical H-bonding
Is too small to occupy a helical position
Forms disulfide bonds that kink the chain
questions.explanation: Proline's side chain cyclizes back onto the backbone nitrogen, so it has no N-H to donate a helical hydrogen bond, and the ring constrains the backbone dihedral angles, disrupting the helix.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The dominant thermodynamic driving force for a soluble protein burying its nonpolar residues in the core is:
options: Formation of new covalent bonds within the core
A decrease in the enthalpy of protein-water hydrogen bonds
The increase in entropy of water released from ordered cages around nonpolar groups
A decrease in the conformational entropy of the polypeptide
questions.explanation: Water forms ordered clathrate-like structures around exposed nonpolar groups. Sequestering those groups releases the ordered water, increasing its entropy; this hydrophobic effect drives folding.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Hemoglobin functions as an assembly of four separate polypeptide chains held together by noncovalent interactions. This arrangement is an example of:
questions.explanation: Quaternary structure refers to the spatial arrangement of multiple folded polypeptide subunits into a functional complex, as in tetrameric hemoglobin.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: In Michaelis-Menten kinetics, Km is defined as:
options: The maximum velocity of the reaction
The substrate concentration at which velocity equals one-half of Vmax
The number of substrate molecules converted per enzyme per second
The substrate concentration at which velocity equals Vmax
questions.explanation: By definition, Km is the substrate concentration that produces a velocity equal to half of Vmax; it reflects the substrate level needed to half-saturate the enzyme.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: For the same substrate, enzyme A has a Km of 0.1 mM and enzyme B has a Km of 5 mM. Which statement is best supported?
options: Enzyme A binds the substrate more tightly (higher apparent affinity)
Enzyme B binds the substrate more tightly
Enzymes A and B must have identical Vmax values
Enzyme B reaches half-maximal velocity at a lower substrate concentration
questions.explanation: A lower Km means half-maximal velocity is reached at a lower substrate concentration, which corresponds to a higher apparent affinity for the substrate.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: An enzyme has Vmax = 120 micromol/min and Km = 4 mM. At a substrate concentration of 12 mM, the initial velocity is closest to:
questions.explanation: Using v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]) = 120(12)/(4 + 12) = 1440/16 = 90 micromol/min.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: On a Lineweaver-Burk (double-reciprocal) plot of 1/v versus 1/[S], the y-intercept equals:
questions.explanation: The linearized equation 1/v = (Km/Vmax)(1/[S]) + 1/Vmax gives a y-intercept of 1/Vmax; the x-intercept is -1/Km and the slope is Km/Vmax.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The ratio kcat/Km is commonly used as a measure of:
options: The equilibrium constant of the reaction
The dissociation constant of an inhibitor
questions.explanation: kcat/Km is the specificity constant; it approximates the second-order rate constant for enzyme meeting substrate at low [S] and is the standard metric of catalytic efficiency.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A reversible inhibitor binds only to the free enzyme's active site, competing with substrate. Compared with the uninhibited reaction, this inhibitor:
options: Increases the apparent Km while leaving Vmax unchanged
questions.explanation: A competitive inhibitor raises the apparent Km (more substrate is needed to reach half-maximal velocity) but Vmax is unchanged because excess substrate can outcompete the inhibitor.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A pure noncompetitive inhibitor binds a site away from the active site with equal affinity to free enzyme and to the enzyme-substrate complex. Relative to no inhibitor, it:
options: Decreases Vmax while leaving Km unchanged
Increases Km while leaving Vmax unchanged
Decreases both apparent Km and Vmax
questions.explanation: Pure noncompetitive inhibition lowers the effective amount of active enzyme, reducing Vmax; because the inhibitor binds E and ES equally, the substrate's apparent Km is unchanged.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: An uncompetitive inhibitor binds only to the enzyme-substrate complex. Its characteristic effect on the apparent kinetic parameters is to:
options: Increase apparent Km while raising Vmax
Decrease both apparent Km and Vmax
Decrease Vmax only, with Km unchanged
questions.explanation: By binding ES and pulling the equilibrium toward more complex formation, an uncompetitive inhibitor lowers the apparent Km, and by sequestering ES it also lowers Vmax; the ratio Vmax/Km stays roughly constant.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which type of reversible inhibition can be fully overcome by adding a large excess of substrate?
questions.explanation: Because a competitive inhibitor and substrate compete for the same site, saturating the enzyme with substrate outcompetes the inhibitor and restores Vmax.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A plot of reaction velocity versus substrate concentration for an allosteric enzyme showing positive cooperativity is best described as:
questions.explanation: Positive cooperativity means substrate binding at one site increases affinity at others, producing an S-shaped (sigmoidal) velocity curve rather than the hyperbola of a simple Michaelis-Menten enzyme.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: In many biosynthetic pathways, the final product accumulates and inhibits the enzyme that catalyzes the first committed step. This regulatory strategy is called:
options: Competitive inhibition of the terminal enzyme
questions.explanation: Feedback inhibition occurs when a pathway's end product allosterically inhibits an early committed step, preventing wasteful overproduction.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Trypsinogen and chymotrypsinogen are catalytically inactive precursors that become active only after specific proteolytic cleavage. Such inactive precursors are called:
questions.explanation: Zymogens (proenzymes) are inactive precursors activated by irreversible proteolytic cleavage, a control mechanism that keeps digestive proteases inert until needed.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A nucleoside differs from a nucleotide in that a nucleoside lacks:
questions.explanation: A nucleoside is a nitrogenous base joined to a sugar; adding one or more phosphate groups converts it to a nucleotide.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: In double-stranded DNA, how do the hydrogen bonds of a guanine-cytosine pair compare with those of an adenine-thymine pair?
options: A G-C pair has three hydrogen bonds and an A-T pair has two
A G-C pair has two and an A-T pair has three
Both pairs have two hydrogen bonds
Both pairs have three hydrogen bonds
questions.explanation: Guanine-cytosine pairs form three hydrogen bonds, whereas adenine-thymine pairs form two, making G-C pairs more stable.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A DNA sample with high GC content will have which melting temperature, and why?
options: Lower Tm, because G-C pairs have only two hydrogen bonds
Lower Tm, because G-C pairs stack poorly
Higher Tm, because A-T pairs resist unwinding
Higher Tm, because each G-C pair is held by three hydrogen bonds
questions.explanation: More G-C content means more three-hydrogen-bond pairs, so more thermal energy is required to separate the strands, raising the melting temperature (Tm).

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The two strands of a DNA double helix are described as antiparallel, meaning:
options: Both strands run in the 5' to 3' direction on the same side
One strand runs 5' to 3' while its complement runs 3' to 5'
The strands are joined covalently end to end
The strands have identical base sequences
questions.explanation: Antiparallel means the two strands run in opposite 5'-to-3' orientations, a geometry required for complementary base pairing in the double helix.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: A biochemical reaction with a negative change in Gibbs free energy (delta G < 0) is best described as:
questions.explanation: A negative delta G indicates an exergonic, thermodynamically spontaneous process; note that spontaneity says nothing about the reaction rate.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Cells drive an endergonic reaction (delta G > 0) forward most commonly by:
options: Coupling it to ATP hydrolysis, a strongly exergonic reaction
Lowering the temperature of the cytosol
Increasing the activation energy of the reaction
questions.explanation: Energetic coupling links an unfavorable reaction to a highly favorable one (typically ATP hydrolysis) so that the summed delta G is negative and the overall process proceeds.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The standard free-energy change (delta G naught prime) of a reaction is a fixed value, but the actual delta G inside a cell depends on:
options: The identity of the enzyme catalyzing the reaction
The activation energy of the reaction
The actual concentrations of reactants and products
questions.explanation: delta G = delta G naught prime + RT ln(Q), so the real free-energy change depends on the reaction quotient Q, which is set by the actual reactant and product concentrations. Enzymes and activation energy affect rate, not delta G.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: For a reaction at standard biochemical conditions with delta G naught prime < 0, the equilibrium constant Keq is:
questions.explanation: Because delta G naught prime = -RT ln(Keq), a negative delta G naught prime requires ln(Keq) > 0, so Keq is greater than 1 (products favored at equilibrium).

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The net yield of glycolysis, per molecule of glucose converted to two pyruvate, is:
questions.explanation: Glycolysis produces 4 ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation but invests 2 ATP in the priming steps, for a net of 2 ATP, plus 2 NADH; no FADH2 is generated.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The main rate-limiting and committed step of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Phosphofructokinase-1, which phosphorylates fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate, catalyzes the committed and principal rate-limiting step of glycolysis.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: During intense exercise, human muscle reduces pyruvate to lactate. The primary purpose of this fermentation step is to:
options: Generate two additional molecules of ATP directly
Regenerate NAD+ so that glycolysis can continue
Produce acetyl-CoA for the citric acid cycle
questions.explanation: Lactate dehydrogenase reoxidizes NADH to NAD+, replenishing the oxidized cofactor that the GAPDH step of glycolysis requires so ATP production can continue anaerobically.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) activity is controlled allosterically. Which combination correctly describes its regulation?
options: Activated by ATP, inhibited by AMP
Activated by citrate, inhibited by fructose-2,6-bisphosphate
Activated by NADH, inhibited by ADP
Activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, inhibited by ATP and citrate
questions.explanation: High ATP and citrate signal abundant energy and inhibit PFK-1, while AMP (low energy) and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate are potent activators, matching flux to cellular energy needs.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Compared with hexokinase, liver glucokinase differs in that it:
options: Has a high Km and is not inhibited by its product glucose-6-phosphate
Has a much lower Km than hexokinase
questions.explanation: Glucokinase has a high Km, so it acts mainly when blood glucose is high, and it is not feedback-inhibited by glucose-6-phosphate, allowing the liver to store excess glucose after a meal.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: In eukaryotic cells, the reactions of the citric acid (Krebs) cycle take place in the:
questions.explanation: All citric acid cycle enzymes are soluble in the mitochondrial matrix, except succinate dehydrogenase, which is embedded in the inner membrane as Complex II.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Per single turn of the citric acid cycle (per acetyl-CoA oxidized), the outputs are:
questions.explanation: One turn generates 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP (or ATP), and releases 2 CO2 as the two-carbon acetyl group is fully oxidized.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which enzyme is generally considered the primary rate-limiting control point of the citric acid cycle, being activated by ADP and inhibited by ATP and NADH?
questions.explanation: Isocitrate dehydrogenase is the key regulatory enzyme of the cycle; it is stimulated by ADP (low energy) and inhibited by ATP and NADH (high energy), tuning flux to energy demand.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate, restoring a citric-acid-cycle intermediate that had been drained for biosynthesis. Reactions that replenish cycle intermediates are termed:
questions.explanation: Anaplerotic ('filling up') reactions replenish depleted citric acid cycle intermediates; the pyruvate carboxylase reaction that regenerates oxaloacetate is the classic example.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The terminal electron acceptor of the mitochondrial electron transport chain is:
questions.explanation: At Complex IV, electrons are passed to molecular oxygen, the terminal acceptor, which is reduced to water; cytochrome c and coenzyme Q are only intermediate carriers.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: ATP synthase produces ATP by harnessing energy from:
options: Direct transfer of phosphate from NADH to ADP
The proton (H+) electrochemical gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane
Oxidation of FADH2 at Complex II
questions.explanation: Electron transport pumps protons into the intermembrane space; the resulting electrochemical gradient (proton-motive force) drives protons back through ATP synthase, powering ATP formation via chemiosmosis.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: FADH2 yields fewer ATP than NADH during oxidative phosphorylation because:
options: FADH2 carries fewer electrons than NADH
FADH2 cannot be reoxidized in the mitochondrion
FADH2 donates its electrons directly to oxygen
Electrons from FADH2 enter at Complex II, bypassing the proton-pumping Complex I
questions.explanation: FADH2 feeds electrons into the chain at Complex II, skipping Complex I, so fewer protons are pumped per electron pair and less ATP is generated compared with NADH.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: 2,4-Dinitrophenol (DNP) is a lipophilic weak acid that shuttles protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane. Its effect on mitochondria is to:
options: Dissipate the proton gradient, so electron transport continues but ATP synthesis falls and heat is released
Increase the ATP yield per molecule of glucose
Inhibit Complex IV and halt oxygen consumption
questions.explanation: As an uncoupler, DNP collapses the proton-motive force. Electron transport (and oxygen use) speeds up to restore the gradient, but the energy is dissipated as heat rather than captured as ATP.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following electron transport chain complexes does NOT pump protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane?
questions.explanation: Complexes I, III, and IV are proton pumps, but Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) only transfers electrons from succinate into the chain via FADH2 and does not translocate protons.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Gluconeogenesis must bypass the irreversible pyruvate kinase step of glycolysis. It does so using:
options: Pyruvate carboxylase together with PEP carboxykinase
A simple reversal of pyruvate kinase
questions.explanation: To convert pyruvate back to phosphoenolpyruvate, pyruvate carboxylase first makes oxaloacetate, which PEP carboxykinase then converts to PEP, bypassing the irreversible pyruvate kinase reaction.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Glycogen phosphorylase, activated during fasting and by epinephrine signaling, catalyzes which reaction?
options: Adding glucose units to a growing glycogen chain
Creating alpha-1,6 branch points in glycogen
Phosphorolytic cleavage of glycogen to release glucose-1-phosphate
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase uses inorganic phosphate to cleave alpha-1,4 glycosidic bonds, releasing glucose-1-phosphate; it degrades rather than synthesizes glycogen.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: In the Cori cycle, lactate produced by exercising muscle travels to the liver, where it is:
options: Fully oxidized to CO2 and water
Converted back to glucose by hepatic gluconeogenesis and returned to muscle
questions.explanation: The Cori cycle shuttles lactate from muscle to liver, where gluconeogenesis regenerates glucose that is sent back to the muscle, effectively exporting the metabolic burden of anaerobic glycolysis.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Each round of fatty acid beta-oxidation shortens the acyl chain by two carbons and produces:
questions.explanation: Each beta-oxidation cycle carries out oxidation, hydration, oxidation, and thiolytic cleavage, yielding one FADH2, one NADH, and one acetyl-CoA while removing two carbons from the chain.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Long-chain fatty acyl-CoA enters the mitochondrial matrix via the carnitine shuttle. Its rate-limiting enzyme, carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT-1), is inhibited by:
questions.explanation: Malonyl-CoA, the committed intermediate of fatty acid synthesis, inhibits CPT-1, preventing simultaneous fatty acid synthesis and oxidation (a futile cycle).

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: The committed and rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase carboxylates acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA using biotin and ATP; this is the committed, rate-limiting step of fatty acid synthesis.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Which statement correctly describes fatty acid synthesis in mammals?
options: It occurs in mitochondria and uses FADH2 as the reductant
It occurs in mitochondria and uses NADH as the reductant
It occurs in the cytosol and uses NADPH as the reductant
It occurs in the cytosol and uses FADH2 as the reductant
questions.explanation: De novo fatty acid synthesis takes place in the cytosol on fatty acid synthase and relies on NADPH (largely from the pentose phosphate pathway) as the reducing agent, unlike mitochondrial beta-oxidation.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Cholesterol embedded in an animal cell membrane affects membrane fluidity by:
options: Always increasing fluidity regardless of temperature
Always decreasing fluidity regardless of temperature
Having no measurable effect on fluidity
Buffering fluidity: restraining it at high temperature and preventing tight packing at low temperature
questions.explanation: Cholesterol acts as a fluidity buffer. At high temperatures it restrains phospholipid movement (reducing fluidity), and at low temperatures it inserts between tails to prevent tight packing (maintaining fluidity).

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: Membrane phospholipids spontaneously form bilayers in aqueous environments because each molecule:
options: Has a hydrophilic phosphate head group and hydrophobic fatty-acyl tails, making it amphipathic
Cannot organize without integral membrane proteins
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are amphipathic: their polar heads face water while their nonpolar tails cluster away from it, so they self-assemble into bilayers with tails in the interior.

questions.subject: Biochemistry
questions.stem: How does the degree of fatty acid saturation in membrane phospholipids influence fluidity at a given temperature?
options: More saturated tails increase fluidity by kinking the chains
Unsaturated fatty acids pack tightly and lower fluidity
Cis double bonds introduce kinks that space chains apart and increase fluidity
Saturation has no effect on membrane fluidity
questions.explanation: Cis double bonds create bends in the acyl chains, preventing tight packing and increasing fluidity; fully saturated chains pack closely and make the membrane more rigid.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: During assembly of the eukaryotic RNA polymerase II preinitiation complex at a promoter containing a TATA box, which event normally occurs first?
options: The TATA-binding protein (TBP) subunit of TFIID binds the TATA box, nucleating complex assembly
RNA polymerase II binds the TATA box directly on its own
TFIIH unwinds the DNA duplex with its helicase activity before any factor binds
A sigma factor recognizes the -10 and -35 promoter elements
questions.explanation: TBP (within TFIID) binds and bends the TATA box, providing the platform for the rest of the general transcription factors and Pol II. Pol II cannot recognize the promoter alone, TFIIH acts later to melt DNA, and sigma factors are bacterial.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A mutation inactivates the enzyme that adds the 7-methylguanosine cap to eukaryotic pre-mRNAs. Which consequence is most directly expected for the affected transcripts?
options: They will be unable to undergo splicing of any internal intron
They will acquire an abnormally long poly-A tail
They will be transcribed in the 3' to 5' direction
They will be recognized poorly by the small ribosomal subunit during cap-dependent translation initiation
questions.explanation: The 5' cap is bound by eIF4E and is required to recruit the 40S subunit for cap-dependent initiation (it also aids stability and export). Splicing is not fully abolished, poly-A addition is independent, and transcription direction is unaffected.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In the first transesterification step of eukaryotic pre-mRNA splicing, a specific intron nucleotide's 2'-OH attacks the 5' splice site to form the lariat intermediate. Which nucleotide performs this attack?
options: A guanosine at the extreme 5' end of the intron
An adenosine within the intron's branch-point sequence
A uracil in the polypyrimidine tract
The first exonic nucleotide 3' of the intron
questions.explanation: The branch-point adenosine's 2'-hydroxyl attacks the 5' splice site, forming the branched lariat in the first transesterification. The other listed residues do not initiate branch formation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: The peptidyl transferase center that catalyzes peptide bond formation during translation is composed chiefly of which component?
options: A protein enzyme located in the small ribosomal subunit
The initiator tRNA occupying the P site
Ribosomal RNA of the large subunit (the ribosome is a ribozyme)
questions.explanation: The catalytic peptidyl transferase activity resides in the large-subunit rRNA, making the ribosome a ribozyme. EF-G drives translocation, not bond formation, and the initiator tRNA is a substrate carrier, not the catalyst.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In E. coli grown with lactose present but glucose absent, the lac operon is transcribed strongly. Which combination best explains this maximal transcription?
options: Allolactose inactivates the repressor while high cAMP-CAP boosts RNA polymerase binding
Allolactose activates the repressor while low cAMP-CAP boosts polymerase binding
Glucose-derived cAMP binds CAP while the repressor remains on the operator
The repressor stays bound to the operator and CAP is absent
questions.explanation: Lactose (via allolactose) inactivates the LacI repressor (induction), and the absence of glucose raises cAMP so cAMP-CAP binds and enhances transcription. This dual positive/negative control gives maximal expression.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: HIV is a retrovirus whose genome is single-stranded RNA. Which packaged enzyme must act first after the genome enters the host cytoplasm?
options: Reverse transcriptase, an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase
A DNA ligase that seals host chromosomal breaks
questions.explanation: Reverse transcriptase copies the ssRNA genome into cDNA before integration into the host genome. RNA-dependent RNA polymerase is used by RNA viruses that replicate RNA from RNA, and host DNA polymerase acts on DNA templates.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Bacteria grown for many generations in heavy 15N medium are shifted to light 14N medium. After one round of replication the DNA forms a single band of intermediate density. After a second round, which result best supports semiconservative replication?
options: Two bands, one heavy and one light
Two equal bands, one intermediate-density and one light-density
questions.explanation: After two rounds, semiconservative replication predicts half the molecules are hybrid (intermediate) and half fully light, giving two equal bands. The all-heavy or heavy+light outcomes are ruled out by the intermediate band seen after round one.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: On the lagging strand, DNA is synthesized as Okazaki fragments. Which pair of activities removes the RNA primers and then joins adjacent fragments, respectively?
options: Primase removes primers; helicase joins fragments
DNA polymerase (via 5' to 3' exonuclease and gap filling) removes primers; DNA ligase joins fragments
Topoisomerase removes primers; primase joins fragments
DNA ligase removes primers; DNA polymerase joins fragments
questions.explanation: In E. coli, DNA polymerase I uses 5' to 3' exonuclease activity to excise RNA primers and fill the gaps, and DNA ligase seals the remaining nick. Ligase joins fragments; it does not remove primers.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A single base substitution changes the mRNA codon GAA (glutamate) to GAG (glutamate). This mutation is best classified as:
questions.explanation: Both GAA and GAG encode glutamate, so the amino acid sequence is unchanged, a silent substitution reflecting codon degeneracy. There is no premature stop, no amino acid change, and no shift in reading frame.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Two pea plants of genotype RrYy are crossed (round R dominant to wrinkled r; yellow Y dominant to green y), with the genes assorting independently. What fraction of offspring are expected to be round AND green?
questions.explanation: Round occurs in 3/4 of offspring and green (yy) in 1/4; multiplying gives 3/4 x 1/4 = 3/16. The 9/16 class is round-yellow and 1/16 is wrinkled-green.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A person with genotype I^A I^B expresses both A and B antigens on their red blood cells. This inheritance pattern is best described as:
questions.explanation: Both alleles are simultaneously and fully expressed (both antigens appear), which defines codominance. Incomplete dominance would produce a blended intermediate rather than both distinct antigens.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A woman who carries an X-linked recessive disorder (X^A X^a) has children with an unaffected man (X^A Y). What is the probability that any given SON is affected?
questions.explanation: Sons receive the Y from their father and one X from their mother; half of the mother's X chromosomes carry the recessive allele, so 1/2 of sons are affected. (Among all children combined, the affected fraction is 1/4.)

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In a family, a trait appears in every generation, affects males and females about equally, and every affected child has at least one affected parent; two unaffected parents never produce an affected child. Which inheritance pattern fits best?
questions.explanation: Appearance in every generation with no skipping, roughly equal sex distribution, and affected children always having an affected parent are hallmarks of autosomal dominant inheritance. Recessive traits skip generations, and mitochondrial traits pass only from mothers.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In Labrador retrievers, the genotype ee at one locus blocks all pigment deposition (yellow coat) regardless of the alleles at the B locus that specify black or brown. A BBee dog is therefore yellow. This masking is an example of:
questions.explanation: The recessive genotype at the E locus hides the phenotypic expression of the B locus, which defines recessive epistasis (one gene masking another). This is not blending, simultaneous expression, or physical linkage.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In a large, randomly mating population at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, an autosomal recessive condition affects 1 in 10,000 individuals. Approximately what fraction of the population are heterozygous carriers?
questions.explanation: With q^2 = 1/10,000, q = 0.01 and p is about 0.99, so carrier frequency 2pq is about 2(0.99)(0.01) = 0.0198, roughly 1 in 50. The 1-in-100 value is just q, and 1-in-10,000 is the affected frequency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which meiotic event is chiefly responsible for producing new combinations of alleles that lie on the SAME chromosome?
options: Crossing over between homologous chromosomes in prophase I
Independent assortment of homologs at metaphase I
Separation of sister chromatids in anaphase II
questions.explanation: Crossing over physically exchanges segments between homologs, recombining alleles located on the same chromosome. Independent assortment shuffles different chromosomes, and anaphase II separates identical sister chromatids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A gamete is found to contain two NON-identical copies of chromosome 21 (the individual's own maternal and paternal homologs). This is most consistent with nondisjunction occurring during:
questions.explanation: Failure of homologous chromosomes to separate in meiosis I produces a gamete carrying two different homologs. Nondisjunction in meiosis II would instead yield two identical sister chromatids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Two genes on the same chromosome yield 20% recombinant offspring in a testcross. The approximate map distance between them is:
questions.explanation: Recombination frequency in percent approximates map distance in centimorgans, so 20% corresponds to about 20 cM. A frequency of 50% would indicate the genes assort independently (unlinked).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which of the following is a REQUIRED condition for a population to remain in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium at a locus?
options: Ongoing gene flow between neighboring populations
No mutation, random mating, no selection, no migration, and a large population
A small population size to minimize variation
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires no mutation, random mating, no selection, no gene flow, and a large (effectively infinite) population. The other choices are evolutionary forces that change allele frequencies.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A random environmental catastrophe kills most of a beetle population, and the few survivors happen to have allele frequencies quite different from the original population. This change in the gene pool is best described as:
options: A population bottleneck, a form of genetic drift
questions.explanation: A drastic, non-selective (random) reduction in population size that alters allele frequencies is a bottleneck, a type of genetic drift. Selection would act on differential fitness, not on chance survival.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Over generations, a bird population comes to have more individuals with very small and very large beaks and fewer with intermediate beaks. This pattern is characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Favoring both extremes at the expense of intermediate phenotypes is disruptive selection. Stabilizing selection favors intermediates, and directional selection shifts the mean toward one extreme.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In malaria-endemic regions, the sickle-cell allele remains common even though homozygotes suffer serious disease. The best explanation is that:
options: Heterozygotes have higher fitness than either homozygote because they resist malaria while avoiding sickle-cell disease
The allele is dominant and always advantageous
Malaria raises the mutation rate toward the sickle allele
Homozygous recessive individuals are the most malaria-resistant and reproduce most
questions.explanation: Heterozygotes (HbA/HbS) are protected against severe malaria while escaping sickle-cell disease, giving them the highest fitness; this heterozygote advantage (balancing selection) maintains the allele. Homozygous HbS individuals are harmed by the disease.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A newly formed river permanently divides one lizard population into two groups that can no longer interbreed, and over time they become distinct species. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Speciation driven by a physical geographic barrier separating populations is allopatric speciation. Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic isolation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Most ATP made during aerobic respiration is produced by ATP synthase, which is powered by a proton gradient. Where is this proton gradient (proton-motive force) generated?
options: Across the plasma membrane, into the extracellular space
Across the outer mitochondrial membrane, into the cytosol
Across the inner mitochondrial membrane, into the intermembrane space
questions.explanation: The electron transport chain pumps protons from the matrix into the intermembrane space across the inner mitochondrial membrane, and ATP synthase harnesses their return. The outer membrane is freely permeable to protons and cannot hold a gradient.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A membrane protein destined for the plasma membrane is synthesized and processed. Which sequence correctly traces its route through the cell?
options: Free cytosolic ribosomes to nucleus to Golgi to plasma membrane
Rough ER to Golgi apparatus to secretory vesicle to plasma membrane
Smooth ER to lysosome to Golgi to plasma membrane
Rough ER to peroxisome to Golgi to plasma membrane
questions.explanation: Membrane and secretory proteins are made on rough ER ribosomes, modified and sorted through the Golgi, and delivered to the plasma membrane in vesicles. Lysosomes and peroxisomes are not steps in this export pathway.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A short-lived cytosolic regulatory protein must be selectively destroyed. Which structure degrades it, and what molecular tag marks it for destruction?
options: The smooth ER; a signal peptide
questions.explanation: Cytosolic regulatory proteins tagged with a polyubiquitin chain are degraded by the 26S proteasome. Mannose-6-phosphate instead targets enzymes to the lysosome, which handles endocytosed and bulk material rather than tagged cytosolic proteins.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which cytoskeletal component both forms the mitotic spindle that segregates chromosomes and serves as the track for motor-protein-driven vesicle transport?
questions.explanation: Microtubules build the mitotic spindle and act as rails for kinesin and dynein moving vesicles. Actin drives cytokinesis and cell motility, and intermediate filaments provide mechanical strength.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Intestinal epithelial cells absorb glucose against its concentration gradient using the SGLT1 cotransporter. What directly powers this uphill glucose movement?
options: Direct ATP hydrolysis by the SGLT1 transporter itself
The inward sodium electrochemical gradient maintained by the Na+/K+ ATPase
Simple diffusion of glucose down its own concentration gradient
The proton gradient produced by the electron transport chain
questions.explanation: SGLT1 is a secondary active transporter: glucose uptake against its gradient is coupled to Na+ flowing down its electrochemical gradient, which the ATP-consuming Na+/K+ pump maintains. SGLT1 does not itself hydrolyze ATP.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A red blood cell is placed in a beaker of pure distilled water. What is the most likely outcome and why?
options: It shrinks (crenates) because water leaves the cell
No net water movement occurs because the solution is isotonic
It shrinks because solutes rush out of the cell
It swells and may lyse because water enters down the osmotic gradient
questions.explanation: Distilled water is hypotonic to the cytoplasm, so water moves into the cell down its water-potential gradient, causing swelling and possible hemolysis. Crenation occurs in hypertonic, not hypotonic, solutions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: For each ATP it hydrolyzes, the electrogenic Na+/K+ ATPase transports:
options: 3 Na+ into the cell and 2 K+ out of the cell
2 Na+ out of the cell and 3 K+ into the cell
3 Na+ out of the cell and 2 K+ into the cell
3 K+ out of the cell and 2 Na+ into the cell
questions.explanation: The pump exports 3 Na+ and imports 2 K+ per ATP, producing a net export of positive charge that helps set the negative resting potential. Reversing the directions or ratios describes no real physiological pump.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which protein, often called the guardian of the genome, can arrest the cell cycle at the G1/S checkpoint to permit DNA repair or, if damage is severe, trigger apoptosis?
questions.explanation: p53 detects DNA damage and halts the cycle at G1/S (through p21 inhibiting cyclin-CDK complexes) or induces apoptosis. Cyclin B drives the G2/M transition, Ras is a proto-oncogene, and telomerase maintains telomeres.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Compared with a cell in G1, a cell that has just completed S phase but has not yet divided has:
options: Half the DNA content, with each chromosome having one chromatid
Double the DNA content, with each chromosome consisting of two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere
Unchanged DNA content and unreplicated chromosomes
Double the chromosome NUMBER, with each chromosome having one chromatid
questions.explanation: S phase replicates DNA, doubling DNA content while keeping the chromosome number constant; each chromosome now has two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere. Chromosome number stays the same until anaphase separates the chromatids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A drug blocks microtubule polymerization, preventing mitotic spindle formation. A dividing cell exposed to it would most likely arrest at:
options: G1, unable to begin DNA synthesis
S phase, unable to replicate DNA
M phase (metaphase), because chromosomes cannot align and separate
questions.explanation: Without a functional spindle, chromosomes cannot align or segregate, so the spindle assembly checkpoint arrests the cell in mitosis (metaphase). DNA synthesis and G1 progression do not depend on spindle microtubules.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which statement best contrasts oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes in cancer development?
options: Both typically require loss-of-function of both alleles to promote cancer
Oncogenes act through a dominant gain-of-function (one hit), whereas tumor suppressors usually require loss-of-function of both alleles (two hits)
Tumor suppressors act via gain-of-function while oncogenes require loss-of-function
Both act recessively at the cellular level and each requires two hits
questions.explanation: Activated oncogenes are dominant gain-of-function changes that need only one hit, while tumor suppressors typically must lose both functional alleles (Knudson two-hit model) to promote cancer. The options that reverse or equate these mechanisms are incorrect.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which feature distinguishes apoptosis from necrosis?
options: Apoptosis causes cell swelling, membrane rupture, and inflammation
Apoptosis is a passive process resulting directly from physical injury
Apoptosis spills cellular contents to recruit immune cells
Apoptosis is a regulated, energy-dependent process featuring cell shrinkage, DNA fragmentation, and apoptotic bodies cleared without inflammation
questions.explanation: Apoptosis is programmed and ATP-dependent, with membrane blebbing and apoptotic bodies engulfed by phagocytes without triggering inflammation. Necrosis, by contrast, involves swelling, rupture, and an inflammatory response.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A cell capable of giving rise to every cell type of the embryo proper AND the extraembryonic tissues such as the placenta is best described as:
questions.explanation: Totipotent cells, such as the zygote and earliest blastomeres, can form all embryonic and extraembryonic tissues. Pluripotent cells form all embryonic germ layers but not the placenta, and multipotent cells are restricted to related lineages.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A bacterium acquires new genes when a bacteriophage injects DNA that the phage had accidentally packaged from a previously infected host bacterium. This mode of horizontal gene transfer is:
questions.explanation: Transfer of bacterial genes carried by a bacteriophage is transduction. Transformation is uptake of naked DNA, conjugation is direct cell-to-cell transfer through a pilus, and binary fission is asexual reproduction rather than gene transfer.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A temperate phage integrates its genome into the host bacterial chromosome, where it is copied along with host DNA for many generations without producing new virions. This state is the:
questions.explanation: An integrated, quietly replicating phage genome (prophage) that produces no virions defines the lysogenic cycle. The lytic cycle instead makes new virions and lyses the host.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which feature is characteristic of prokaryotic, but not typical eukaryotic, gene expression?
options: Adding 5' caps and poly-A tails to mRNAs before translation
Exporting mRNA out of a nucleus before translation begins
Removing introns from most transcripts using a spliceosome
Organizing related genes into an operon transcribed as a single polycistronic mRNA
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes cluster functionally related genes in operons, producing a single polycistronic mRNA, and translate it while still transcribing (no nucleus). Capping, poly-A tailing, nuclear export, and spliceosomal splicing are eukaryotic features.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Antibiotics that target the bacterial ribosome can kill bacteria without harming human cells mainly because:
options: Bacterial ribosomes (70S, made of 30S and 50S) differ structurally from eukaryotic cytoplasmic ribosomes (80S, made of 40S and 60S)
Human cells have no ribosomes at all
The antibiotics physically cannot cross the human plasma membrane
Human cells rapidly export all antibiotics through the Golgi
questions.explanation: Selective toxicity arises because the bacterial 70S ribosome differs structurally from the eukaryotic 80S cytoplasmic ribosome, so the drug binds bacterial but not human cytoplasmic ribosomes. Human cells clearly do possess ribosomes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Why are viruses considered obligate intracellular parasites?
options: They possess ribosomes but lack any genome
They can perform glycolysis but not the citric acid cycle
They lack ribosomes and the metabolic machinery to make proteins and ATP, so they must use a host cell's machinery to replicate
They reproduce only by binary fission inside host cells
questions.explanation: Viruses have no ribosomes and no energy-generating or biosynthetic machinery, so they must commandeer host cell machinery to replicate. They do not carry out glycolysis or reproduce by binary fission.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: The rapid depolarizing upstroke of a neuronal action potential is caused mainly by:
options: Voltage-gated K+ channels opening, letting K+ exit the cell
Voltage-gated Na+ channels opening, letting Na+ enter the cell
The Na+/K+ ATPase running in reverse
Cl- channels opening, letting Cl- enter the cell
questions.explanation: The upstroke results from voltage-gated Na+ channels opening, allowing Na+ to rush in down its electrochemical gradient and drive the membrane toward positive potentials. K+ efflux causes the later repolarization, not the upstroke.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: When an action potential reaches an axon terminal at a chemical synapse, what is the immediate trigger for synaptic vesicles to fuse and release neurotransmitter?
options: Influx of Ca2+ through voltage-gated calcium channels
Efflux of K+ from the terminal
Binding of neurotransmitter to postsynaptic receptors
Reuptake of neurotransmitter into the presynaptic terminal
questions.explanation: Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, and the resulting Ca2+ influx triggers vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release. Postsynaptic receptor binding is a downstream event, and reuptake terminates signaling.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis, rising blood levels of thyroid hormone suppress release of TRH and TSH. This regulatory arrangement is an example of:
questions.explanation: The end product (thyroid hormone) inhibits its own upstream releasing signals, stabilizing hormone levels, which defines negative feedback. Positive feedback would amplify the output, as in oxytocin during labor.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Compared with peptide hormones, steroid hormones generally:
options: Bind cell-surface receptors and act through second messengers such as cAMP
Are stored in secretory vesicles for rapid, brief release
Are lipid-soluble, cross the plasma membrane, and bind intracellular receptors that act as transcription factors
Produce faster-onset but shorter-lasting effects than peptide hormones
questions.explanation: Lipophilic steroid hormones diffuse across the membrane and bind intracellular or nuclear receptors that alter gene transcription, giving slower onset but longer-lasting effects. Peptide hormones, by contrast, use surface receptors and second messengers and act quickly.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: After a carbohydrate-rich meal raises blood glucose, which hormonal response is expected?
options: Increased glucagon secretion, stimulating hepatic glycogen breakdown
Increased insulin secretion, promoting glucose uptake by muscle and adipose tissue and glycogen synthesis
Decreased insulin secretion to conserve glucose
questions.explanation: High blood glucose stimulates pancreatic beta cells to secrete insulin, which increases glucose uptake (via GLUT4) and glycogen synthesis, lowering blood glucose. Glucagon and epinephrine instead act to raise glucose during fasting or stress.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Which structure normally functions as the heart's pacemaker, spontaneously depolarizing to set the heart rate?
questions.explanation: The SA node in the right atrium has the fastest intrinsic rate of spontaneous depolarization and sets the heart rate. The AV node delays conduction, and the His-Purkinje system distributes the impulse to the ventricles.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Hard-exercising skeletal muscle produces CO2 and lactic acid, lowering the local pH in its capillaries. By the Bohr effect, this change causes hemoglobin to:
options: Bind oxygen more tightly, raising its affinity
Show unchanged O2 affinity, since pH does not affect hemoglobin
Release CO2 and take up more O2
Lower its O2 affinity, shifting the dissociation curve rightward and unloading more O2 to the tissue
questions.explanation: Increased CO2 and H+ (lower pH) decrease hemoglobin's oxygen affinity, shifting the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve to the right and promoting O2 unloading where metabolically active tissue needs it most.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Protein digestion begins in the stomach. Which enzyme, secreted as an inactive zymogen and activated by the stomach's acidic pH, starts this process?
options: Pepsin, activated from pepsinogen by low pH
Pancreatic lipase, activated by bile salts
questions.explanation: Chief cells secrete pepsinogen, which the acidic gastric environment converts to active pepsin to begin protein digestion in the stomach. Trypsin acts in the small intestine, amylase digests carbohydrates, and lipase digests fats.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: On a second exposure to a virus, the body rapidly produces large amounts of antibody. Which cells secrete these antibodies, and what type of immunity is this?
options: Plasma cells derived from B lymphocytes; humoral immunity
questions.explanation: Antibodies are secreted by plasma cells, which are differentiated B lymphocytes, in the humoral arm of adaptive immunity; memory B cells enable the fast, strong secondary response. Cytotoxic T cells kill infected cells directly (cell-mediated immunity).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: Cytotoxic (CD8+) T cells detect virus-infected body cells by recognizing viral peptides displayed on:
options: MHC class II molecules found only on antigen-presenting cells
Secreted antibodies circulating in the blood
MHC class I molecules on the surface of the infected cell
questions.explanation: CD8+ cytotoxic T cells recognize antigen presented on MHC class I, which all nucleated cells express and which displays endogenous (for example viral) peptides. MHC class II presents to CD4+ helper T cells on antigen-presenting cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In the sliding filament model of skeletal muscle contraction, the sarcomere shortens because:
options: Myosin heads bind actin and pull the thin filaments toward the sarcomere's center, without the filaments themselves shortening
The individual actin and myosin filaments each shorten
The A band shortens while the I band lengthens
Calcium directly cleaves bonds within the myosin thick filament
questions.explanation: Cross-bridge cycling slides the thin (actin) filaments past the thick (myosin) filaments toward the M line, so the sarcomere shortens (I band and H zone narrow) while the filaments and the A band keep their length.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: A mid-cycle surge of luteinizing hormone (LH) in the human menstrual cycle directly triggers:
options: Ovulation (release of the secondary oocyte from the mature follicle)
The start of the follicular phase
questions.explanation: The LH surge, driven by high estrogen via positive feedback, induces ovulation near mid-cycle. Menstruation follows falling progesterone, and the LH surge does not cause implantation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.stem: In response to elevated plasma osmolarity, antidiuretic hormone (ADH, vasopressin) acts on the nephron primarily to:
options: Increase water permeability of the collecting duct by inserting aquaporins, concentrating the urine
Increase sodium reabsorption in the distal tubule via the Na+/K+ pump
Stimulate potassium secretion in the collecting duct
Decrease water reabsorption to produce dilute urine
questions.explanation: ADH inserts aquaporin-2 channels into the collecting duct, increasing water reabsorption and concentrating the urine, which restores normal plasma osmolarity. Sodium and potassium handling in that segment is instead governed largely by aldosterone.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
We praise the surgeon whose patient recovers and condemn the one whose patient dies, yet the two may have performed identical operations with identical skill. The difference lies not in what they did but in what chance made of it. This unsettling fact—that our moral verdicts track outcomes we do not control—has long troubled philosophers who wish morality to be fair. If justice requires that we be judged only for what lies within our power, then judging people by results looks like a kind of superstition dressed as ethics.

Yet the alternative is stranger still. Imagine a world in which we assessed only intentions, ignoring what actually happened. The reckless driver who kills a child and the equally reckless driver who happens to hit no one would merit identical blame. Most of us cannot accept this; the death seems to matter. We feel, however confusedly, that the world's cooperation is part of what we answer for.

Perhaps the lesson is that our moral practices were never built for a universe indifferent to our deserving. We evolved to respond to consequences because consequences are what we can see. To demand that ethics confine itself to the will alone is to ask a practical instrument to behave like a proof. The honest conclusion may be that we live with a contradiction we cannot resolve—only manage.
questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the central claim of the passage?
options: Ethics should judge people solely by their intentions, since outcomes lie beyond their control.
Because our moral judgments track outcomes we cannot control, the tension between fairness and practice can be managed but not resolved.
The example of the two surgeons shows that surgical skill is irrelevant to moral blame.
Morality is a superstition that thoughtful people should abandon.
questions.explanation: The passage argues both that judging by outcome seems unfair and that judging by intention alone is unacceptable, ending with the claim that we live with a contradiction we can only manage. Judging by intention alone is the view the author rejects, not endorses. The surgeon example shows that outcome, not skill, drives the verdict. And the author calls outcome-judging a 'superstition dressed as ethics' but never urges abandoning morality itself.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the possibility of removing the role of chance from moral judgment is best described as:
options: confident that focusing on intention will succeed
indifferent to whether morality is fair
resigned to a contradiction that can be managed but not solved
outraged at the unfairness of all moral judgment
questions.explanation: The closing sentence—'we live with a contradiction we cannot resolve—only manage'—signals resignation. He is not confident intention-only judging will work (he calls it unacceptable), he plainly cares about fairness so he is not indifferent, and his measured, analytical tone is not outrage.

questions.stem: In context, the phrase 'a kind of superstition dressed as ethics' refers to the practice of:
options: assigning moral blame according to outcomes a person did not control
believing that surgeons can predict which patients will recover
insisting that only intentions, never results, should be judged
questions.explanation: The sentence criticizes 'judging people by results' as superstition. Insisting on intentions only is the opposite stance the author later considers. The other options do not describe what the phrase denotes.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly supports which of the following statements?
options: A morality confined strictly to the will would match our actual moral responses more closely than current practice does.
The reckless driver who kills a child is less blameworthy than one who happens to hit no one.
Justice has never required that people be judged only for what lies within their power.
Our moral practices developed in response to observable consequences rather than to abstract requirements of fairness.
questions.explanation: The author states 'We evolved to respond to consequences because consequences are what we can see' and that ethics was 'never built' for a fairness-indifferent universe. A will-only morality would clash with our responses, since the author says we cannot accept intention-only judging. The reckless-driver claim is reversed from the passage. And justice requiring judgment only for what is in our power is presented as a live premise, not one denied outright.

questions.stem: Which scenario best illustrates the author's idea that 'the world's cooperation is part of what we answer for'?
options: Two chefs use identical recipes; both dishes turn out equally well, and both are praised.
A student who studies hard earns a high grade, while a lazy student earns a low one.
Two people fire guns recklessly into the air; one bullet happens to strike and kill a passerby, and only that shooter is charged with homicide.
A doctor refuses to operate and is blamed for the patient's death.
questions.explanation: The author's point is that identical conduct earns different blame depending on chance outcomes. In the gunfire case the reckless acts are the same; only where a bullet lands differs, yet blame diverges—exactly the moral-luck structure. The chefs' outcomes do not diverge, the student case ties reward to effort (something controlled), and the refusing doctor involves different conduct, not identical conduct with different luck.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most undermine the author's claim that we cannot accept judging by intention alone?
options: Across many cultures, people assign exactly the same blame to a reckless driver whether or not anyone is harmed.
Surveys show that most people blame the surgeon whose patient dies more than the one whose patient lives.
Legal systems often impose harsher penalties when reckless conduct causes death.
People often feel guilt even for harms they could not have foreseen.
questions.explanation: The author claims 'Most of us cannot accept' judging by intention alone. Evidence that people actually do blame equally regardless of outcome directly contradicts that empirical premise. The other findings all reinforce the author's picture that outcomes drive our judgments.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
Historians like to say they follow the evidence, but the evidence has already chosen them. What survives in an archive is rarely what once mattered most; it is what someone thought worth keeping, could afford to store, and managed to protect from fire, damp, and the recycling of old paper into new. A tax ledger endures because a government guarded it; a laborer's grief leaves no receipt. The past that reaches us is thus filtered twice—first by those who recorded, then by those who preserved.

This should make us cautious about the confident narratives that fill textbooks. When a chronicle falls silent about the poor, we may be tempted to conclude the poor were quiet or content. More often they were simply unwritten. The absence of a record is not the record of an absence.

None of this means history is fiction. It means the historian's first task is to interrogate the shape of the silence—to ask not only what the documents say but why these documents, and not others, lie before us. A skilled reader of the past treats every surviving page as a survivor, bearing the marks of the accidents that spared it. Only then can the archive be read against its own grain, and the missing be, however faintly, restored.
questions.stem: The main idea of the passage is that:
options: history is essentially fiction, since the records cannot be trusted
textbooks are reliable because they draw on documents that have survived
the poor of the past were generally content, since they left few complaints
surviving records are a biased sample shaped by who could record and preserve them, so historians must scrutinize what is missing
questions.explanation: The passage argues the past reaches us 'filtered twice' and that the historian's first task is to 'interrogate the shape of the silence.' History as fiction is explicitly denied ('None of this means history is fiction'). Trusting textbooks runs against the author's call for caution, and the claim that the poor were content is the very error he warns against.

questions.stem: By 'The absence of a record is not the record of an absence,' the author means that:
options: missing documents prove that nothing important happened
the lack of a written trace does not establish that the thing itself never existed
historians should ignore any event that lacks written evidence
records are always destroyed on purpose
questions.explanation: The line warns against inferring nonexistence from silence—for example, concluding the poor were content when they were merely 'unwritten.' Taking silence as proof of nonexistence inverts the meaning, ignoring undocumented events is the opposite of the author's advice, and the sentence makes no claim about deliberate destruction.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
Historians like to say they follow the evidence, but the evidence has already chosen them. What survives in an archive is rarely what once mattered most; it is what someone thought worth keeping, could afford to store, and managed to protect from fire, damp, and the recycling of old paper into new. A tax ledger endures because a government guarded it; a laborer's grief leaves no receipt. The past that reaches us is thus filtered twice—first by those who recorded, then by those who preserved.

This should make us cautious about the confident narratives that fill textbooks. When a chronicle falls silent about the poor, we may be tempted to conclude the poor were quiet or content. More often they were simply unwritten. The absence of a record is not the record of an absence.

None of this means history is fiction. It means the historian's first task is to interrogate the shape of the silence—to ask not only what the documents say but why these documents, and not others, lie before us. A skilled reader of the past treats every surviving page as a survivor, bearing the marks of the accidents that spared it. Only then can the archive be read against its own grain, and the missing be, however faintly, restored.
questions.stem: The author's attitude toward historians who say they 'follow the evidence' is best described as:
options: respectful of the phrase as a complete account of their method
contemptuous, regarding their work as worthless
gently critical, treating the phrase as naive without dismissing history itself
indifferent to how historians describe their work
questions.explanation: The author qualifies the phrase ('the evidence has already chosen them') yet insists 'history is not fiction'—a critical but not dismissive stance. Treating the phrase as complete misses the critique, calling the work worthless overshoots into contempt, and his evident concern rules out indifference.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly implies that a textbook statement such as 'the peasants of this region were peaceful and content' should be treated as:
options: suspect, because the silence may reflect a lack of surviving records rather than actual contentment
well founded, because contented people rarely leave records of complaint
certainly false, since peasants were always miserable
irrelevant, because the lives of peasants cannot be studied at all
questions.explanation: The author warns that when a chronicle 'falls silent about the poor, we may be tempted to conclude the poor were quiet or content' when 'more often they were simply unwritten.' Treating the claim as well founded commits exactly that error; declaring it certainly false overstates in the opposite direction; and calling peasant lives unstudiable contradicts the call to read 'against the grain.'

questions.stem: Which research practice best exemplifies the author's recommendation to read the archive 'against its own grain'?
options: Accepting a royal chronicle's account of a reign at face value
Counting only the events explicitly described in surviving letters
Discarding any document whose author is unknown
Using a wealthy merchant's account book to reconstruct the lives of the unnamed servants it mentions only in passing
questions.explanation: Reading 'against the grain' means extracting evidence a source did not set out to provide—here, servants' lives drawn from a merchant's ledger. Accepting a chronicle at face value and counting only explicit events both read only with the grain, and discarding anonymous documents throws away evidence rather than reinterpreting it.

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the author's claim that survival depends on power and resources rather than on importance?
options: A study finds that ancient documents survive at random, independent of who created them.
A survey shows that the overwhelming majority of documents surviving from a given era were produced by governments, churches, and wealthy estates.
Historians report that peasant diaries are as common in archives as royal decrees.
Most surviving records turn out to describe the most historically significant events.
questions.explanation: The author claims records survive because someone 'could afford to store' and 'managed to protect' them. A skew toward powerful, well-resourced producers confirms this. Random survival and peasant diaries being as common as royal decrees both undercut the claim, while records tracking significance supports the rival view that importance, not resources, governs survival.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
A familiar defense of democratic authority holds that we obey the law because we have consented to it. But few of us have consented to anything. We were born into a state we did not choose, under laws written before we existed, and the ballot we cast—if we cast one—more often loses than wins. To call this consent stretches the word past use.

Defenders reply that consent may be tacit: by remaining, by using the roads, by enjoying the protection of the courts, we accept the bargain. Yet tacit consent is a curious thing, for it asks agreement of people who were never offered a genuine choice to refuse. The person who cannot afford to emigrate has not consented to stay any more than a passenger consents to a voyage by failing to leap from the ship.

Perhaps democratic legitimacy rests on something firmer than consent. We might obey not because we agreed but because the procedure that made the law gave everyone a fair chance to be heard, and fairness, not agreement, is what binds. On this view a law can be legitimate even for those who voted against it, provided the contest was open and the losers may argue again tomorrow. Legitimacy would then be a property of the process, not a promise anyone made.
questions.stem: The central argument of the passage is that:
options: citizens have genuinely consented to their governments by choosing to remain
no government can ever be legitimate
consent poorly explains why we must obey the law, and legitimacy may instead rest on the fairness of the law-making procedure
people should obey only the laws they personally voted for
questions.explanation: The author dismantles both express and tacit consent, then proposes that legitimacy is 'a property of the process, not a promise anyone made.' Genuine consent by remaining is the view he rejects; the claim that no government can be legitimate overstates, since he offers a basis for legitimacy; and obeying only laws one voted for is contradicted by his claim that a law can bind even those who voted against it.

questions.stem: The comparison of a citizen to 'a passenger [who] consents to a voyage by failing to leap from the ship' is meant to show that:
options: remaining in a country is a clear and deliberate act of agreement
emigration is easy for anyone who truly objects to the government
citizens are like prisoners with no rights at all
staying cannot count as genuine consent when leaving is not a real option
questions.explanation: The analogy targets tacit consent: not leaping overboard is no real choice, just as not emigrating (when one 'cannot afford to') is not real agreement. Treating staying as deliberate agreement or emigration as easy reverses the point, and the prisoner comparison overstates beyond what the analogy claims.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the notion of 'tacit consent' is best described as:
options: skeptical, treating it as a strained way to preserve a favored conclusion
enthusiastic, regarding it as the strongest defense of authority
neutral, presenting it without any evaluation
bewildered, admitting he cannot understand it
questions.explanation: He calls tacit consent 'a curious thing' that 'asks agreement of people who were never offered a genuine choice to refuse'—clear skepticism. Enthusiasm and neutrality both miss his critique, and treating the idea as unintelligible mistakes a pointed objection for confusion.

questions.stem: On the author's proposed account of legitimacy, a law is binding provided that:
options: every citizen personally agreed to it
it was produced by a fair, open procedure in which all could be heard and the losers may contest it again
a majority benefits from it, regardless of how it was passed
it has remained in force for a long time
questions.explanation: The author writes that a law can be legitimate 'provided the contest was open and the losers may argue again tomorrow.' Personal agreement by all is the consent view he abandons, and majority benefit or long duration introduce criteria the passage never endorses.

questions.stem: Which law would the author most likely consider legitimate even for those who oppose it?
options: A tax passed by a ruler who permits no public debate and jails critics
A decree issued in secret and never subjected to challenge
A tax enacted after open debate, which the minority who lost may campaign to repeal in the next session
A law that a large majority happens to like but that was imposed without any hearing
questions.explanation: The author's procedural test requires an open contest with the losers free to 'argue again tomorrow'—exactly the third scenario. Suppressing debate and issuing secret decrees fail the openness condition, and a law imposed without any hearing fails the requirement of a fair chance to be heard, popularity notwithstanding.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most weaken the author's claim that fairness of procedure, rather than agreement, is what binds citizens?
options: People report feeling bound by a law only when they personally agreed to it, no matter how fair the procedure that produced it.
Most citizens cannot afford to emigrate.
Ballots more often lose than win.
Governments existed before any particular citizen was born.
questions.explanation: The author asserts 'fairness, not agreement, is what binds.' Evidence that people feel bound only by what they agreed to directly contradicts that. The other three are premises the author himself uses to attack consent, so they support rather than weaken his case.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
To forgive is widely thought a virtue, and the person who cannot let go of a wrong is pitied as a prisoner of the past. Yet there is something troubling in the demand that victims forgive. Forgiveness, if it means anything, is freely given; an obligation to forgive would make it a debt the wronged party owes the wrongdoer, which inverts the moral order. The one who was harmed would owe something to the one who did the harm.

Consider, too, what resentment protects. To resent an injury is to insist that it mattered, that one was owed better. A victim who forgives too quickly may be abandoning a rightful claim, even colluding in the pretense that nothing serious occurred. Lingering anger can be a form of self-respect.

This is not a brief for endless grudges. Resentment held too long can corrode the one who holds it, and forgiveness, when it comes unforced, may heal both parties. The point is narrower: forgiveness earns its moral shine precisely because it is not required. Praise it as generous, hope for it, even work toward it—but do not command it. To make forgiveness a duty is to strip it of the freedom that made it worth admiring, and to add, to the injury already suffered, the fresh burden of a debt.
questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with arguing that:
options: victims have a moral duty to forgive those who wrong them
resentment is always destructive and should be abandoned quickly
wrongdoers are owed forgiveness once they apologize
forgiveness is admirable precisely because it is freely given, and so should be hoped for but not required
questions.explanation: The author concludes that forgiveness 'earns its moral shine precisely because it is not required' and urges us to 'hope for it... but do not command it.' A duty to forgive is the view he opposes; he denies resentment is always destructive (it 'can be a form of self-respect'); and he never claims wrongdoers are owed forgiveness upon apology.

questions.stem: In saying 'Lingering anger can be a form of self-respect,' the author suggests that resentment can:
options: affirm that one was genuinely wronged and deserved better treatment
prove that the wrongdoer has apologized sincerely
guarantee that the relationship will be repaired
show that the victim has already forgiven the offense

questions.stem: The passage implies that praising forgiveness as generous is:
options: inconsistent with also treating it as morally optional
fully compatible with declining to make it a duty
a way of secretly obligating the victim
possible only if the wrongdoer deserves it

questions.stem: The author's stance toward forgiveness itself, as opposed to the demand that victims forgive, is best described as:
options: hostile—he regards forgiveness as a weakness
dismissive—he thinks it makes no real difference
indifferent—he takes no position on its worth
appreciative—he values it while denying it can be obligatory

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most challenge the author's position?
options: Victims who forgive freely often report a sense of relief.
Victims who are socially expected to forgive tend to recover better and report no loss of self-respect.
Some grudges last for many years.
questions.explanation: The author warns that obligating forgiveness adds 'a fresh burden' and can cost the victim self-respect. Evidence that expected (quasi-obligatory) forgiveness improves recovery without harming self-respect undercuts that worry.
