questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage:
Every age seems to believe it has fallen from a better one. The Roman moralist lamented the luxury that had softened his ancestors' stern virtue; the medieval scholar mourned the lost wisdom of antiquity; the modern citizen recalls a childhood when neighbors trusted one another and the world made sense. The golden age is always just behind us, glowing in a light that never quite reaches the present.

It is worth asking why this backward glance is so persistent. One answer is that memory is a poor historian. It discards the ordinary and the painful, keeping the vivid and the sweet, so the past is remembered as better simply because it is remembered selectively. Another answer is political: those who wish to resist change find no argument so powerful as the claim that we are betraying an inheritance.

The danger is not nostalgia itself, which is harmless enough as a private mood. The danger is nostalgia mistaken for history—the conviction that a real and superior past has been stolen, and might be seized back if only the present were torn down. Golden ages, examined closely, dissolve into the same mixture of misery and hope as our own time. A people that forgets this may sacrifice a workable present to a past that never existed.
questions.stem: The passage argues that:
options: every past age really was better than the one that followed it
the recurring belief in a lost golden age is usually an illusion, and mistaking it for real history is dangerous
nostalgia is harmful in all its forms and should be suppressed
memory is a reliable guide to what the past was truly like
questions.explanation: The author shows the golden-age belief recurs in every era, traces it to selective memory and politics, and warns of 'nostalgia mistaken for history.' That past ages were truly better is contradicted ('Golden ages... dissolve into the same mixture of misery and hope'); nostalgia is not condemned in all forms (as a mood it is 'harmless'); and memory is called 'a poor historian.'

questions.passage:
Every age seems to believe it has fallen from a better one. The Roman moralist lamented the luxury that had softened his ancestors' stern virtue; the medieval scholar mourned the lost wisdom of antiquity; the modern citizen recalls a childhood when neighbors trusted one another and the world made sense. The golden age is always just behind us, glowing in a light that never quite reaches the present.

It is worth asking why this backward glance is so persistent. One answer is that memory is a poor historian. It discards the ordinary and the painful, keeping the vivid and the sweet, so the past is remembered as better simply because it is remembered selectively. Another answer is political: those who wish to resist change find no argument so powerful as the claim that we are betraying an inheritance.

The danger is not nostalgia itself, which is harmless enough as a private mood. The danger is nostalgia mistaken for history—the conviction that a real and superior past has been stolen, and might be seized back if only the present were torn down. Golden ages, examined closely, dissolve into the same mixture of misery and hope as our own time. A people that forgets this may sacrifice a workable present to a past that never existed.
questions.stem: Which of the following would the author most likely identify as the dangerous form of nostalgia?
options: A grandmother fondly recalling songs from her youth
A historian documenting both the hardships and comforts of a past century
A traveler enjoying an old photograph of a city
A movement urging citizens to dismantle current institutions in order to reclaim a national greatness it says was stolen
questions.explanation: The author's danger is nostalgia mistaken for history and used to justify tearing down the present to seize back a supposed lost age—precisely the movement described. The grandmother and the traveler exemplify the harmless 'private mood,' and the historian is engaged in sober history, not nostalgia.

questions.stem: Which finding would most weaken the author's argument that golden ages are illusions produced by selective memory?
options: People in ancient Rome, the Middle Ages, and today all believed their own era had declined.
Rigorous measures of trust, safety, and prosperity show that one specific past period was genuinely better than the present by every standard.
Individuals tend to remember pleasant experiences more readily than unpleasant ones.
Politicians frequently invoke a lost past to resist change.
questions.explanation: The author claims golden ages 'dissolve' under scrutiny into the same mix of misery and hope as the present. Hard evidence that a real period was measurably better by every standard would show at least one golden age was no illusion. questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: When conservators strip a centuries-old varnish from a painting, they describe their work as a return: the recovery of colors the artist first laid down. Yet the notion of a single original state is more convenient than true. A painting is not a fixed object but a record of its own aging—the slow ambering of resin, the fine web of cracks, the softening of once-strident hues. To a viewer in 1650, a freshly finished canvas may have looked garish; the very qualities we now prize as "authentic patina" were, to the painter's contemporaries, merely the surface not yet settled. When we scrub a work back to its supposed birth, we do not uncover the past so much as manufacture a version of it that flatters modern eyes, which have been trained by bright reproductions and gallery lighting to equate cleanliness with truth. This is not an argument against all restoration; a canvas left to decay tells us nothing. It is an argument for humility. The restorer's chemicals are as much an interpretation as the critic's prose, and every interpretation chooses what to sacrifice. To pretend otherwise—to speak of "the original" as if it waited, intact, beneath the grime—is to disguise a decision as a discovery.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the central claim of the passage?
options: Restoration inevitably harms paintings and should be abandoned.
The idea of recovering a painting's single original state conceals an interpretive choice.
Modern audiences appreciate cleaned paintings because their taste is superior to that of the past.
A painter's contemporaries reliably disliked their newly finished canvases.
questions.explanation: The passage argues that 'the original' is not a fixed fact waiting to be found but a construct produced by the restorer's choices—cleaning 'manufactures a version' rather than uncovering one. Option A overstates: the author explicitly says this 'is not an argument against all restoration.' Option C distorts the point about trained modern eyes into a claim of superior taste. Option D turns a tentative 'may have looked garish' into a reliable rule.

questions.stem: In context, the phrase "to disguise a decision as a discovery" most nearly means:
options: to present an interpretive choice as though it were the neutral uncovering of a pre-existing fact
to conceal a genuine scientific breakthrough from rival conservators
to hide accidental damage caused during the cleaning process
to mistake a real discovery for an arbitrary decision
questions.explanation: Throughout, the author equates the restorer's 'chemicals' with an 'interpretation'—a chosen 'decision'—that gets misrepresented as if the true painting simply 'waited, intact, beneath the grime' (a 'discovery'). Option A captures this. B and C invent breakthroughs and damage the text never mentions. D reverses the phrase: the author's worry is decisions posing as discoveries, not the other way around.

questions.stem: The author would most likely agree that what we prize as "authentic patina":
options: was deliberately produced by the original artist to add depth
should always be preserved regardless of a painting's condition
results from the painting's aging rather than the artist's original intention
is admired today for the same reasons the painter's contemporaries admired it
questions.explanation: Patina is described as part of the painting's 'record of its own aging' (ambering resin, cracks, softened hues)—not something the artist set out to make. Option C follows. A contradicts this by crediting the artist. B ignores the author's acceptance that some intervention is needed ('a canvas left to decay tells us nothing'). D is refuted directly: contemporaries saw these qualities as an unsettled surface, not something to admire.

questions.stem: Which situation best parallels the author's view of restoration?
options: A historian discovers a lost letter that resolves a long-standing dispute.
A scientist repeats an experiment and confirms the original result.
A museum installs brighter bulbs so visitors can see the works more clearly.
An editor modernizes an old novel's spelling and punctuation, then calls the result the author's true text.
questions.explanation: The author's key move is that an interpretive alteration gets rebranded as recovery of the 'true' original. Option D reproduces exactly this: a present-day change presented as the author's authentic text. A is a genuine discovery (the very thing the author says restoration is NOT). B is neutral confirmation. C alters viewing conditions, not the object, and makes no false claim to recovering an original.

questions.stem: The author most likely notes that "a canvas left to decay tells us nothing" in order to:
options: concede that some intervention is necessary, distinguishing his critique from a rejection of all restoration
argue that gradual decay is the only authentic fate for a painting
prove that restorers always improve the works they clean
suggest that viewers should study only perfectly preserved works
questions.explanation: This clause is a concession that keeps the argument balanced: the author is criticizing the rhetoric of 'the original,' not opposing restoration itself. Option A states this. B says the opposite (decay is bad, per the author). C overclaims a benefit the author never grants—he says restoration is an interpretation that 'sacrifices,' not a guaranteed improvement. D is unsupported.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most weaken the author's argument?
options: Many viewers say they prefer the look of aged, uncleaned paintings.
Chemical analysis can objectively reconstruct a painting's original pigments, and cleaned works consistently match that reconstruction.
Restorers frequently disagree about which solvents to use on a given work.
Old varnish continues to darken steadily over the centuries.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the claim that a single 'original state' cannot be objectively recovered—cleaning only 'manufactures' a flattering version. Option B directly undercuts that: if the original is objectively reconstructable and cleaned works match it, then restoration really is discovery, not disguised decision. A supports the author's subjectivity point. C reinforces that restoration involves interpretive choices. D supports the aging-over-time claim.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: Ask a classroom what a poem "really means," and someone will reach for the poet's biography as if it were a key cut to fit the lock. If we knew what the writer suffered, the reasoning goes, we would know what the lines intend. But a poem is not a diary entry in disguise, and the poet is not its final authority. Once a line is written and released, it belongs to the language it is made of, and language carries more than any single mind can command. A word the poet chose for its sound may ring, for a reader, with a meaning the poet never weighed; an image meant as private grief may open, in another era, onto public catastrophe. None of this makes interpretation lawless. The text sets limits: a reading that ignores what the words plainly say is not bold but wrong. What the text does not do is hand us a hidden intention to be retrieved, like treasure, by those who know the poet's life. Biography can enrich a reading, but it cannot settle one. The meanings a poem sustains are the meanings its words will bear—tested against the poem, not against the person who happened to write it.
questions.stem: The passage's central argument is that:
options: Knowing a poet's biography is the surest route to a poem's true meaning.
Because language is uncontrollable, every interpretation of a poem is equally valid.
A poem's meaning is governed by what its words can support, not by the poet's private intentions.
Poems are essentially disguised diaries recording the poet's suffering.
questions.explanation: The closing lines make the thesis explicit: meaning is 'tested against the poem, not against the person who happened to write it,' and readings are limited to 'the meanings its words will bear.' Option C captures both halves. A and D are the very positions the author rejects. B goes too far—the author insists interpretation is 'not lawless' because 'the text sets limits.'

questions.stem: The image of "a key cut to fit the lock" is used to represent the assumption that:
options: biographical knowledge precisely unlocks a poem's single intended meaning
poems are deliberately locked against ordinary readers
only trained scholars are equipped to interpret difficult poetry
a poet designs each poem to conceal a private secret
questions.explanation: The key/lock image immediately precedes the paraphrase 'if we knew what the writer suffered... we would know what the lines intend'—biography as the exact instrument that opens a poem's intended meaning. Option A matches. B and C introduce ideas of exclusion or scholarly gatekeeping the passage never raises. D describes intentional concealment by the poet, which the author does not claim.

questions.stem: The author insists that interpretation is "not lawless" primarily to establish that:
options: the poet's intention remains the ultimate limit on a poem's meaning
only a single interpretation of any poem can be correct
biography must be excluded entirely from serious reading
the words of the text constrain which readings are acceptable
questions.explanation: The 'not lawless' sentence is immediately explained: 'The text sets limits: a reading that ignores what the words plainly say is not bold but wrong.' The constraint is the words themselves—option D. A restores the poet's intention as the limit, which the author denies. B overstates 'limits' into a single correct reading, ignoring that a poem can 'sustain' several. C contradicts 'biography can enrich a reading.'

questions.stem: A novelist insists her book is "only about a family, not about politics," yet readers find a political allegory the words clearly support. The author would most likely conclude that:
options: the novelist's statement settles the matter, so the political reading is invalid
the political reading is legitimate if the words sustain it, despite the novelist's denial
any reading a reader proposes is equally valid, whatever the words say
the dispute can be resolved only by studying the novelist's biography
questions.explanation: The author holds that meaning is what 'its words will bear,' not what the author intends—so a text-supported reading stands even against the writer's denial (option B). A makes the author the 'final authority,' which the passage rejects. C ignores the 'text sets limits' constraint. D reintroduces biography as decisive, contradicting 'it cannot settle one.'

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward biographical information is best described as:
options: hostile—biography has no legitimate place in reading poems
reverent—biography is the highest form of criticism
measured—biography can be useful but is not decisive
indifferent—biography neither helps nor hinders a reading
questions.explanation: The line 'Biography can enrich a reading, but it cannot settle one' gives both sides: useful yet not authoritative. Option C matches precisely. A is too extreme (the author grants enrichment). B inverts his view. D denies any value, but he explicitly says biography can 'enrich.'

questions.stem: Which finding would most strengthen the author's claim that a poem can carry meanings the poet never weighed?
options: Documented cases exist in which readers across different eras drew coherent, text-supported meanings that the poet had explicitly disavowed.
A survey finds that most readers want to learn about a poet's life.
Poets are shown to revise their poems heavily before publishing.
Poems with clearly stated intentions are found to sell better.
questions.explanation: The claim is that a poem's words can 'bear' legitimate meanings beyond the poet's intention. Option A is direct evidence: readings the poet denied, yet 'coherent' and 'text-supported' across eras, show the text carries meaning independent of authorial intent. B concerns reader curiosity, not what the text can mean. C is about drafting. D is about sales, both irrelevant to whether words outrun intention.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: The traveler who seeks the "real" village—untouched, unposed, going about its ancient business—is chasing something the search itself dissolves. As soon as a place is valued for being authentic, that value becomes a resource, and residents, being neither foolish nor rich, learn to supply it. The festival once held for the gods is held again for the season of visitors; the craft once made for use is made for sale. Critics call this corruption, a fall from some purer state. But the purer state is largely a fiction of the outsider, who imagines that before the tourists came, the village existed in a timeless present, doing forever what it had always done. In truth every living culture has always borrowed, adapted, and performed itself for others. What the tourist names "authentic" is simply a version of the place that has not yet learned to anticipate his gaze—and it stops being that the moment he arrives to admire it. The honest response is not to mourn a lost purity that never was, but to notice that the demand for authenticity is itself a force that shapes what it claims only to observe. The visitor is never a neutral witness. He is part of the scene he came to see.
questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the main idea of the passage?
options: Tourism inevitably corrupts once-pure traditional cultures.
Villages should refuse to perform their traditions for outsiders.
Authentic cultures can be preserved if tourists behave respectfully.
The very demand for authenticity reshapes the cultures tourists hope to find unchanged.
questions.explanation: The thesis is that 'the demand for authenticity is itself a force that shapes what it claims only to observe'—option D. A adopts the 'corruption' framing the author explicitly rejects (there was no pure state to fall from). B prescribes a policy the author never recommends. C assumes a preservable authenticity, but the author says the sought-after authenticity 'stops being that the moment he arrives.'

questions.stem: By calling the visitor "part of the scene he came to see," the author most nearly means that the tourist:
options: is welcomed as an honorary member of the community
alters the very culture he believes he is only observing
ought to take an active role in local festivals
encounters nothing but staged performances and never any real life
questions.explanation: The phrase caps the claim that the visitor 'is never a neutral witness'—his presence and demand change what he observes. Option B states this. A misreads 'part of the scene' as social acceptance. C turns a descriptive point into advice. D overstates: the author says the tourist shapes the scene, not that everything he sees is fake.

existed once but can no longer be recovered
still survives in villages tourists have not yet reached
is preserved only in festivals still held for the gods

B concedes it once existed, which the author denies. C and D locate a surviving purity somewhere, contradicting the claim that all living cultures were always changing and performing.

questions.stem: Toward critics who call staged authenticity "corruption," the author's attitude is best described as:
options: fully in agreement that tourism corrupts traditions
admiring of their defense of a vanishing purity
skeptical, because they assume a timeless past that never existed
unconcerned, treating their view as irrelevant to cultural study

A and B side with the critics. D is wrong because he engages their view seriously rather than dismissing it as irrelevant.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: The traveler who seeks the "real" village—untouched, unposed, going about its ancient business—is chasing something the search itself dissolves. As soon as a place is valued for being authentic, that value becomes a resource, and residents, being neither foolish nor rich, learn to supply it. The festival once held for the gods is held again for the season of visitors; the craft once made for use is made for sale. Critics call this corruption, a fall from some purer state. But the purer state is largely a fiction of the outsider, who imagines that before the tourists came, the village existed in a timeless present, doing forever what it had always done. In truth every living culture has always borrowed, adapted, and performed itself for others. What the tourist names "authentic" is simply a version of the place that has not yet learned to anticipate his gaze—and it stops being that the moment he arrives to admire it. The honest response is not to mourn a lost purity that never was, but to notice that the demand for authenticity is itself a force that shapes what it claims only to observe. The visitor is never a neutral witness. He is part of the scene he came to see.
questions.stem: A city advertises a neighborhood as an "undiscovered" arts district; within a year rents rise and studios become boutiques marketing the district's edginess. This best illustrates the author's point that:
options: genuine authenticity can survive commercial pressure intact
tourists usually fail to notice cultural change around them
critics are right to label such change a corruption
naming something authentic turns it into a resource that reshapes it
questions.explanation: This mirrors the passage's core mechanism: once a place is 'valued for being authentic, that value becomes a resource' that transforms it (option D). A contradicts the example, in which authenticity does not survive. B introduces obliviousness the scenario doesn't show. C endorses the 'corruption' framing the author rejects.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: We tell the successful that they earned it, and mostly they believe us. The belief is not merely vanity; it is the natural conclusion of a story in which effort is visible and luck is not. A person remembers the early mornings, the declined invitations, the years of unglamorous work—and these memories are true. What the same person cannot remember, because it was never experienced as an event, is the accident of being born where such effort would be rewarded, to parents who could afford to wait for it to pay off. Luck of this kind does not feel like luck. It feels like the weather: the invisible medium in which striving happens, not a factor within it. This is why appeals to "privilege" so often fail to persuade those who have it. They are being asked to credit a cause they have no memory of, against the vivid evidence of their own exertion. The point is not that effort is a myth—it plainly matters—but that a society which can see only effort will mistake a lottery for a level field. It will moralize outcomes that were half decided before the striving began, and it will be puzzled, and offended, when the losers decline to accept the verdict as just.
questions.stem: The passage's main idea is that:
options: Because luck is not experienced as an event, the successful tend to credit their success to effort alone.
Effort plays no genuine role in success; outcomes are decided purely by luck.
The successful deliberately hide the role privilege played in their lives.
Society should stop rewarding effort in order to become fair.
questions.explanation: The essay hinges on effort being 'visible' while luck 'does not feel like luck,' so the successful over-credit effort (option A). B overshoots—the author says effort 'plainly matters.' C implies deliberate concealment, but the author's point is that the advantage was 'never experienced as an event,' i.e., invisible, not hidden on purpose. D is a policy prescription the author never makes.

questions.stem: By saying advantageous luck "feels like the weather," the author most nearly means that it is experienced as:
options: something that changes unpredictably from one day to the next
the background condition of striving rather than a distinct cause of success
a force entirely beyond anyone's power to influence
an influence that affects everyone in a community equally
questions.explanation: The author defines the simile immediately: weather is 'the invisible medium in which striving happens, not a factor within it.' Option B restates this. A seizes on weather's unpredictability, which is not the author's point. C imports uncontrollability, and D imports uniform impact—neither is the intended sense of 'invisible medium.'

questions.stem: According to the author, appeals to "privilege" tend to fail because they:
options: remind the privileged that they never truly worked hard
rely on data showing privilege has no measurable effect
ask people to credit a cause they never consciously experienced, against vivid memories of their effort
assume the successful reject all forms of social criticism
questions.explanation: The passage states the reason directly: people 'are being asked to credit a cause they have no memory of, against the vivid evidence of their own exertion' (option C). A is false—the author affirms the effort was real. B reverses the author's view (he holds privilege matters greatly). D is an unsupported generalization about the successful.

questions.stem: The author's stance toward the belief that success is simply "earned" is best described as:
options: contemptuous of anyone who works hard for success
fully supportive of the belief as stated
indifferent to whether outcomes are fair
critical of it, while still granting that effort matters
questions.explanation: The author challenges the 'earned it' story yet concedes 'the point is not that effort is a myth—it plainly matters.' That balance is option D. A misreads his critique of the belief as contempt for hard workers. B endorses the belief he is undermining. C ignores that fairness ('a level field,' 'just') is his central concern.

questions.stem: Which case best illustrates a society that "mistakes a lottery for a level field"?
options: A lottery winner chooses to donate the winnings to charity.
A firm judges its hiring fair because its top performers all worked hard, overlooking that only well-resourced applicants could afford the unpaid internships needed to qualify.
A student who did not study fails an exam.
A company rewards employees strictly according to measured output.
questions.explanation: The author's phrase means treating an outcome shaped by hidden advantage as if it were a fair contest. Option B fits exactly: the firm sees only effort while an unnoticed resource barrier decided who could even compete. A merely plays on the word 'lottery.' C is a case where effort genuinely determined the result. D describes an output-based reward with no hidden-advantage element, so it does not illustrate the confusion.

It will moralize outcomes that were half decided before the striving began, and it will be puzzled, and offended, when the losers decline to accept the verdict as just.
questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most weaken the author's argument?
options: Most successful people report that they worked very hard to succeed.
Many privileged people fail despite the advantages they were given.
When starting circumstances are held equal, differences in effort still account for nearly all the variation in who succeeds.
People generally dislike being told that they are privileged.
questions.explanation: The argument claims that unseen luck 'half decides' outcomes before striving begins. Option C attacks this: if effort explains nearly all success even after equalizing circumstances, luck is not doing the decisive work the author assigns it. A is consistent with the author (effort feels vivid). B shows advantage isn't sufficient but not that it isn't a major factor. D restates a claim the author already makes.

questions.subject: CARS
questions.passage: There is no such thing as a free platform. When a service costs the user nothing, the user is not the customer; he is the inventory. What the company sells is his attention, and attention, unlike money, cannot be budgeted, because we do not feel it leaving us. A person who would never hand a stranger twenty dollars will hand over an hour without noticing, and an hour, compounded across a life, is the more expensive gift. The design follows from the economics. A product paid for in attention must be engineered to extract as much of it as possible, which means it must be pleasant enough to return to and unsatisfying enough that we never quite stop. Contentment is bad for business; a mild, renewable restlessness is ideal. We should therefore be skeptical when such products describe themselves in the language of connection and community, as though their purpose were our flourishing. Their purpose is our engagement, and the two coincide only by accident. This is not a conspiracy—no one need intend harm—but a logic, and a logic is harder to resist than a villain, because it works through the ordinary incentives of everyone involved. To use these tools well, we must first refuse the story they tell about themselves.
questions.stem: The central argument of the passage is that:
options: Digital platforms are secretly run by people who intend to harm their users.
Users should pay money for platforms so that the companies behave ethically.
Attention and money are economically identical resources.
Free platforms are built to maximize users' attention, so their claims to serve connection deserve skepticism.
questions.explanation: The essay ties design to economics ('a product paid for in attention must be engineered to extract as much of it as possible') and concludes we should 'be skeptical' of the connection/community story—option D. A contradicts 'This is not a conspiracy—no one need intend harm.' B is never proposed. C is refuted: attention 'unlike money, cannot be budgeted.'

questions.stem: In saying the user "is the inventory," the author most nearly means that the user is:
options: the product being sold—through his attention—rather than the paying customer
a passive consumer who accepts whatever the platform offers
a stored resource that the company tends to neglect
an unpaid employee who works on the platform's behalf
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts customer and inventory: 'the user is not the customer; he is the inventory. What the company sells is his attention.' The user is the thing sold—option A. B captures passivity but misses the 'sold' relationship. C reads 'inventory' as neglect. D casts the user as a worker, but the passage frames him as the product, not labor.

questions.stem: The claim that "contentment is bad for business" rests on the idea that:
options: satisfied users demand refunds from the platform
a fully satisfied user would stop returning, reducing the attention the platform can sell
the platform earns money directly from users' happiness
contented users end up paying the platform more money
questions.explanation: Since revenue comes from harvesting attention, the product must be 'unsatisfying enough that we never quite stop'; a content user would stop returning and stop generating sellable attention (option B). A invokes refunds irrelevant to a free service. C and D contradict the model—the platform profits from continued engagement, not from happiness or from users paying.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the platforms' language of "connection and community" is best described as:
options: accepting, since he believes connection is their true aim
outraged, because he sees their makers as deliberately cruel
skeptical, treating it as a self-serving story that hides the real incentive
neutral, since he takes no position on the platforms' motives
questions.explanation: The author urges we 'be skeptical' of this language and end by saying we must 'refuse the story they tell about themselves,' since their real purpose is 'engagement' (option C). A takes the story at face value. B overstates into deliberate cruelty, which he denies ('not a conspiracy'). D is wrong—he clearly takes a position on their incentives.

questions.stem: By the author's reasoning, which redesign would run most against a free platform's economic logic?
options: A feed that refreshes endlessly with fresh items.
Notifications timed to pull users back throughout the day.
Rewards for streaks of consecutive daily visits.
A feature that helps users finish what they came to do and then log off satisfied.
questions.explanation: The logic requires keeping users unsatisfied and returning ('never quite stop'). A feature that leaves users satisfied and logged off (option D) reduces harvestable attention, cutting against that logic. A, B, and C all extend engagement—endless feeds, re-engaging notifications, and streak rewards—so they align with the economic incentive rather than opposing it.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: An atom of an element in its ground state has the electron configuration [Ar]3d^10 4s^2 4p^3. Based on its position in the periodic table, which of the following best describes this element?
options: It is a metalloid in period 4 that commonly forms a -3 anion
It is a transition metal that most readily forms a +2 cation
It is a noble gas with a completely filled valence shell
It is an alkaline earth metal in period 4
questions.explanation: The configuration has 33 electrons (18 + 10 + 2 + 3), which is arsenic (As), a period-4 metalloid in group 15. With 5 valence electrons (4s^2 4p^3), it can gain 3 to reach a noble-gas configuration, forming As^3-. The filled 3d^10 is not a valence subshell here.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following correctly ranks the atomic radii of the neutral atoms Na, Mg, Al, and P from largest to smallest?
questions.explanation: Across a period (left to right) atomic radius decreases because effective nuclear charge increases while electrons enter the same shell. For period-3 elements Na, Mg, Al, P the order of decreasing radius is Na > Mg > Al > P.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: The first four successive ionization energies (in kJ/mol) of an element are approximately 738, 1451, 7733, and 10540. To which group does this element most likely belong?
questions.explanation: The large jump occurs between the 2nd and 3rd ionization energies (1451 to 7733), indicating that removing the third electron breaks into a noble-gas core. Thus the element has 2 valence electrons and belongs to Group 2. (These are the values for Mg.)

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which molecule has a trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry?
questions.explanation: NH3 has a central N with three bonding pairs and one lone pair (four electron domains, tetrahedral electron geometry), giving trigonal pyramidal molecular geometry. BF3 is trigonal planar, CH4 is tetrahedral, and CO2 is linear.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of the sulfur tetrafluoride molecule (SF4), which has one lone pair on the central sulfur atom?
questions.explanation: SF4 has 5 electron domains (4 bonding + 1 lone pair). The lone pair occupies an equatorial position in the trigonal bipyramidal arrangement to minimize repulsion, giving a seesaw molecular geometry.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is nonpolar despite containing polar bonds?
questions.explanation: CCl4 has four polar C–Cl bonds arranged tetrahedrally and symmetrically, so the bond dipoles cancel and the molecule is nonpolar. H2O (bent), NH3 (pyramidal), and CHCl3 (asymmetric) all have net dipole moments.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: How many grams of oxygen gas are required to completely combust 16 g of methane (CH4, molar mass 16 g/mol) according to CH4 + 2 O2 -> CO2 + 2 H2O?
questions.explanation: 16 g CH4 is 1 mol. The stoichiometry requires 2 mol O2 per mol CH4, so 2 mol O2 = 2 x 32 g/mol = 64 g of O2.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: When 3 mol of H2 react with 3 mol of N2 to form ammonia (N2 + 3 H2 -> 2 NH3), which is the limiting reagent and how many moles of NH3 form?
questions.explanation: The reaction needs 3 mol H2 per 1 mol N2. With 3 mol H2, only 1 mol N2 can react, leaving 2 mol N2 in excess, so H2 is limiting. 3 mol H2 x (2 mol NH3 / 3 mol H2) = 2 mol NH3.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A compound is found to contain 40.0% carbon, 6.7% hydrogen, and 53.3% oxygen by mass. What is its empirical formula? (Atomic masses: C = 12, H = 1, O = 16)
questions.explanation: Moles per 100 g: C = 40.0/12 = 3.33, H = 6.7/1 = 6.7, O = 53.3/16 = 3.33. Dividing by 3.33 gives C1H2O1, so the empirical formula is CH2O.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A rigid 2.0 L container holds an ideal gas at 300 K and 1.0 atm. If the temperature is raised to 600 K while volume is held constant, what is the new pressure?
questions.explanation: At constant volume and moles, P/T is constant (Gay-Lussac's law). Doubling the absolute temperature from 300 K to 600 K doubles the pressure to 2.0 atm.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A container holds a mixture of 2.0 mol He, 3.0 mol Ne, and 5.0 mol Ar at a total pressure of 4.0 atm. What is the partial pressure of neon?
questions.explanation: Mole fraction of Ne = 3.0 / (2.0 + 3.0 + 5.0) = 0.30. Partial pressure = mole fraction x total pressure = 0.30 x 4.0 atm = 1.2 atm.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Two gases, hydrogen (H2, molar mass 2 g/mol) and oxygen (O2, molar mass 32 g/mol), are at the same temperature. What is the ratio of the root-mean-square speed of H2 to that of O2?
questions.explanation: RMS speed is inversely proportional to the square root of molar mass at the same temperature. The ratio is sqrt(M_O2 / M_H2) = sqrt(32/2) = sqrt(16) = 4, so H2 : O2 = 4 : 1.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Under which set of conditions is a real gas expected to deviate MOST from ideal-gas behavior?
questions.explanation: Real gases deviate most from ideality at high pressure (molecules are close, so their finite volume and attractions matter) and low temperature (low kinetic energy makes intermolecular attractions significant). Low P and high T give the most ideal behavior.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For a certain reaction, ΔH = -40 kJ/mol and ΔS = -100 J/(mol·K). At what temperature does the reaction change from spontaneous to nonspontaneous?
options: The reaction is spontaneous at all temperatures
questions.explanation: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS = 0 at the crossover. T = ΔH/ΔS = (-40000 J/mol)/(-100 J/(mol·K)) = 400 K. Below 400 K, ΔG < 0 (spontaneous); above 400 K it becomes nonspontaneous.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Given the reactions: (1) C(s) + O2(g) -> CO2(g), ΔH = -394 kJ; (2) CO(g) + 1/2 O2(g) -> CO2(g), ΔH = -283 kJ. What is ΔH for C(s) + 1/2 O2(g) -> CO(g)?
questions.explanation: By Hess's law, target = reaction (1) minus reaction (2): ΔH = (-394) - (-283) = -111 kJ. Reversing reaction (2) flips its sign to +283, and adding to -394 gives -111 kJ.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following processes has a NEGATIVE change in entropy (ΔS < 0) for the system?
options: Sublimation of dry ice (solid CO2 to gas)
Condensation of water vapor into liquid
questions.explanation: Condensation converts a high-entropy gas into a lower-entropy liquid, so ΔS < 0. Sublimation, dissolving a solid, and boiling all increase disorder and give ΔS > 0.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For the exothermic reaction A -> B, the bond energies are such that ΔH = -50 kJ/mol. If the activation energy of the forward reaction is 30 kJ/mol, what is the activation energy of the reverse reaction (B -> A)?
questions.explanation: ΔH = Ea(forward) - Ea(reverse). So Ea(reverse) = Ea(forward) - ΔH = 30 - (-50) = 80 kJ/mol. The reverse reaction (endothermic) has a higher activation barrier.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: The standard enthalpy of formation (ΔHf°) of an element in its most stable form at standard conditions is:
options: Equal to its bond dissociation energy
questions.explanation: By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its standard state (e.g., O2 gas, C as graphite) is zero, because forming it from itself involves no change.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For a reaction with rate law rate = k[A]^2, if the concentration of A is tripled, by what factor does the reaction rate change?
questions.explanation: The reaction is second order in A. Rate is proportional to [A]^2, so tripling [A] multiplies the rate by 3^2 = 9.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: An experiment on the reaction 2 A + B -> products yields these initial-rate data: when [A] doubles (B constant) the rate doubles; when [B] doubles (A constant) the rate quadruples. What is the overall order of the reaction?
questions.explanation: Doubling [A] doubles the rate, so first order in A. Doubling [B] quadruples the rate (2^2), so second order in B. Overall order = 1 + 2 = 3.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A reactant decays by first-order kinetics with a half-life of 10 minutes. What fraction of the original reactant remains after 30 minutes?
questions.explanation: For first-order decay, each half-life reduces the amount by half. 30 minutes = 3 half-lives, so the fraction remaining is (1/2)^3 = 1/8.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which statement about a catalyst is correct?
options: It increases the rate by raising the temperature of the reaction
It shifts the equilibrium position toward the products
It increases the equilibrium constant of the reaction
It lowers the activation energy for both the forward and reverse reactions
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy, speeding up both forward and reverse reactions equally. It does not change ΔG, Keq, or the equilibrium position; it only helps equilibrium be reached faster.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For a first-order reaction, which plot yields a straight line?
questions.explanation: The integrated first-order rate law is ln[A] = -kt + ln[A]0, so a plot of ln[A] versus time is linear with slope -k. A zero-order reaction gives linear [A] vs t; second order gives linear 1/[A] vs t.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For the equilibrium N2(g) + 3 H2(g) <-> 2 NH3(g) (exothermic), which change will shift the equilibrium toward MORE ammonia production?
options: Increasing the volume of the container
Increasing the pressure by decreasing volume
questions.explanation: By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas (2 mol NH3 vs 4 mol reactants), favoring NH3. Increasing volume, raising temperature (exothermic), or removing N2 all shift toward reactants.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: The equilibrium constant Kc for a reaction is 4.0 x 10^-3 at 25 °C. What does this value indicate about the reaction at equilibrium?
options: The reaction favors products, with products greatly exceeding reactants
The reaction favors reactants, with reactants exceeding products
The reaction is at exactly 50% completion
The reaction has reached its maximum rate
questions.explanation: A Keq much less than 1 (here 4.0 x 10^-3) means the equilibrium lies to the left, so reactant concentrations exceed product concentrations at equilibrium.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For the reaction A(g) <-> 2 B(g) with Kc = 4.0, a 1.0 L vessel initially contains 1.0 M A and no B. Which expression correctly represents the equilibrium condition using the change variable x (where x = M of A consumed)?
questions.explanation: Using an ICE table: [A] = 1.0 - x, [B] = 2x (two B per A consumed). Kc = [B]^2/[A] = (2x)^2/(1.0 - x) = 4.0. The B concentration is squared because of its stoichiometric coefficient.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: At a certain temperature, the reaction quotient Q for a system is found to be greater than the equilibrium constant K. In which direction will the reaction proceed to reach equilibrium?
options: In the reverse direction, forming more reactants
In the forward direction, forming more products
It is already at equilibrium; no net change occurs
questions.explanation: When Q > K, there are too many products relative to equilibrium, so the reaction shifts in the reverse direction (toward reactants) to reduce Q until Q = K.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: What is the pH of a 0.010 M solution of the strong acid HCl?
questions.explanation: HCl is a strong acid and dissociates completely, so [H+] = 0.010 M = 1.0 x 10^-2 M. pH = -log(1.0 x 10^-2) = 2.0.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A weak acid HA has a Ka of 1.0 x 10^-5. What is the approximate pH of a 0.10 M solution of HA?

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A buffer is prepared with 0.20 M acetic acid (pKa = 4.74) and 0.20 M sodium acetate. What is the pH of this buffer?
questions.explanation: By the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation, pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]). With equal concentrations, log(1) = 0, so pH = pKa = 4.74.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following combinations would produce an effective buffer solution?
options: Equal moles of HCl and NaCl
0.5 mol acetic acid and 0.25 mol NaOH in solution
Equal moles of NaOH and NaCl
questions.explanation: Adding 0.25 mol NaOH to 0.5 mol acetic acid neutralizes half the acid, producing 0.25 mol acetate and leaving 0.25 mol acetic acid — a weak acid/conjugate base mixture that buffers. The other options contain only strong acid/base or non-buffering salts.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: During the titration of a weak acid with a strong base, what is true at the half-equivalence point?
options: The pH equals the pKa of the weak acid
All the weak acid has been neutralized
The solution contains only conjugate base
questions.explanation: At the half-equivalence point, exactly half the weak acid has been converted to its conjugate base, so [HA] = [A-]. By Henderson–Hasselbalch, pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: When titrating a weak acid with a strong base (e.g., NaOH), the pH at the equivalence point is:
options: Equal to the pKa of the acid
questions.explanation: At the equivalence point of a weak acid–strong base titration, the solution contains the conjugate base of the weak acid, which hydrolyzes water to produce OH-, making the pH greater than 7 (basic).

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: How many milliliters of 0.20 M NaOH are needed to completely neutralize 40 mL of 0.10 M H2SO4 (a diprotic acid)?
questions.explanation: Moles H2SO4 = 0.040 L x 0.10 M = 0.0040 mol. Being diprotic, it requires 2 x 0.0040 = 0.0080 mol OH-. Volume NaOH = 0.0080 mol / 0.20 M = 0.040 L = 40 mL.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A solution has a pOH of 3.0 at 25 °C. What is the hydronium ion concentration [H3O+]?

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following is the conjugate base of the dihydrogen phosphate ion, H2PO4^-?
questions.explanation: A conjugate base is formed by removing one proton (H+). Removing H+ from H2PO4^- gives HPO4^2-. H3PO4 would be the conjugate acid (adding a proton).

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: The Ksp of AgCl is 1.8 x 10^-10. What is the molar solubility of AgCl in pure water?
questions.explanation: AgCl <-> Ag+ + Cl-, so Ksp = s^2 where s is the molar solubility. s = sqrt(1.8 x 10^-10) = 1.34 x 10^-5 M.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: The molar solubility of a salt MX2 in pure water is s. What is the correct expression for its solubility product Ksp?
questions.explanation: MX2 <-> M^2+ + 2 X-. At equilibrium [M^2+] = s and [X-] = 2s. Ksp = [M^2+][X-]^2 = (s)(2s)^2 = 4s^3.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: How would the molar solubility of the slightly soluble salt CaF2 change if it were dissolved in a 0.10 M NaF solution instead of pure water?
options: It would increase due to the common-ion effect
It would decrease due to the common-ion effect
It would decrease because NaF is a strong acid
questions.explanation: By the common-ion effect, the presence of F- (from NaF) shifts the CaF2 dissolution equilibrium (CaF2 <-> Ca^2+ + 2 F-) toward the solid, decreasing the molar solubility of CaF2.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A solution contains 1.0 x 10^-3 M Ca^2+ and 1.0 x 10^-3 M SO4^2-. If Ksp for CaSO4 is 2.4 x 10^-5, will a precipitate form?
options: Cannot be determined without the temperature
questions.explanation: Q = [Ca^2+][SO4^2-] = (1.0 x 10^-3)(1.0 x 10^-3) = 1.0 x 10^-6. Since Q (1.0 x 10^-6) < Ksp (2.4 x 10^-5), the solution is unsaturated and no precipitate forms.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: In a galvanic (voltaic) cell, oxidation and reduction occur at which electrodes, respectively?
options: Oxidation at the cathode; reduction at the anode
Oxidation at the anode; reduction at the cathode
questions.explanation: By definition, oxidation always occurs at the anode and reduction always occurs at the cathode (mnemonic: 'an ox' and 'red cat'). This holds for both galvanic and electrolytic cells.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Given the standard reduction potentials Cu^2+ + 2e- -> Cu (E° = +0.34 V) and Zn^2+ + 2e- -> Zn (E° = -0.76 V), what is the standard cell potential of a galvanic cell made from these half-cells?
questions.explanation: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode. Cu^2+ has the higher (more positive) reduction potential, so it is reduced (cathode) and Zn is oxidized (anode). E°cell = 0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10 V.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: For a spontaneous galvanic cell reaction, which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: ΔG° = -nFE°cell. For a spontaneous reaction, ΔG° must be negative, which requires E°cell to be positive. Thus E°cell > 0 and ΔG° < 0 for a spontaneous galvanic cell.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: How does an electrolytic cell differ fundamentally from a galvanic cell?
options: In an electrolytic cell, the cathode is positive and no external power is needed
An electrolytic cell uses electrical energy to drive a nonspontaneous reaction
An electrolytic cell has a positive cell potential without an external source
In an electrolytic cell, oxidation occurs at the cathode
questions.explanation: An electrolytic cell requires an external power source to drive a nonspontaneous reaction (E°cell < 0, ΔG° > 0). A galvanic cell produces electricity from a spontaneous reaction. Oxidation still occurs at the anode in both.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: During the electrolysis of molten NaCl, a constant current of 5.0 A is passed for 1930 seconds. Approximately how many moles of sodium metal are deposited at the cathode? (F = 96500 C/mol e-)
questions.explanation: Charge = I x t = 5.0 A x 1930 s = 9650 C. Moles of electrons = 9650/96500 = 0.10 mol. Na+ + e- -> Na requires 1 e- per Na, so 0.10 mol Na is deposited.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: According to the Nernst equation, if the concentration of the product ions in a galvanic cell increases while reactant ion concentrations stay fixed, the cell potential Ecell will:
questions.explanation: The Nernst equation is Ecell = E°cell - (RT/nF)lnQ. Increasing product concentrations raises Q, and since Q appears with a negative sign, Ecell decreases below E°cell.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: What is the oxidation state of chromium in the dichromate ion, Cr2O7^2-?
questions.explanation: Oxygen is -2, and there are 7 oxygens: 7 x (-2) = -14. The overall charge is -2, so 2(Cr) + (-14) = -2, giving 2(Cr) = +12, so each Cr is +6.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A galvanic cell has E°cell = +0.76 V and transfers 2 moles of electrons. Using ΔG° = -nFE° with F = 96500 C/mol, what is ΔG° for this reaction?
questions.explanation: ΔG° = -nFE° = -(2)(96500 C/mol)(0.76 V) = -146,680 J ≈ -147 kJ. The negative value confirms a spontaneous reaction.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following bonds is the most polar (has the greatest difference in electronegativity)?
questions.explanation: Fluorine is the most electronegative element, and hydrogen is relatively low, giving H–F the largest electronegativity difference among these bonds. This makes H–F the most polar.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following species is expected to have the shortest bond length between the two central atoms?
options: All C–C bonds are the same length
questions.explanation: Bond length decreases as bond order increases because more shared electron pairs pull the nuclei closer. A triple bond (ethyne) is shorter and stronger than a double or single C–C bond.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: A sample of an ideal gas occupies 5.0 L at 2.0 atm and 300 K. If it is compressed to 2.5 L and heated to 600 K, what is the new pressure?

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: When a solute is dissolved in a solvent, the resulting solution shows a lower vapor pressure, elevated boiling point, and depressed freezing point compared to the pure solvent. These are examples of:
questions.explanation: Colligative properties depend on the number of dissolved solute particles, not their identity. Vapor-pressure lowering, boiling-point elevation, and freezing-point depression are the classic colligative properties.

questions.subject: General Chemistry
questions.stem: An endothermic reaction has a positive ΔS. Which statement about its spontaneity is correct?
options: It is spontaneous at all temperatures
It is nonspontaneous at all temperatures
It becomes spontaneous only at sufficiently high temperatures
It becomes spontaneous only at sufficiently low temperatures
questions.explanation: With ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0, ΔG = ΔH - TΔS becomes negative only when the TΔS term outweighs ΔH, i.e., at high temperatures. So the reaction is spontaneous only at sufficiently high T.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A molecule contains one hydroxyl group, one ketone, and one carboxylic acid group. Which group is cited as the suffix (the principal characteristic group) in its IUPAC name?
options: Whichever group happens to have the lowest locant
questions.explanation: IUPAC seniority ranks carboxylic acid above ketone above alcohol. The most senior group is named as the suffix; the others become prefixes or lower-priority citations.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A nitrogen atom is bonded directly to a carbonyl carbon (a C(=O)–N linkage). This functional group is best classified as:
questions.explanation: An amide is defined by a nitrogen bonded to a carbonyl carbon. An amine lacks the carbonyl, an imine has a C=N, and a nitrile has a C≡N triple bond.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Consider the carboxylic acid with the condensed structure CH3CH2CH(CH3)COOH. What is its correct IUPAC name?
questions.explanation: The longest chain that includes the COOH (C1) is four carbons (butanoic acid), with a methyl branch on C2. 'Propanoic' would ignore the longest-chain rule.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A hydrocarbon has the molecular formula C6H10. How many degrees of unsaturation does it contain?
questions.explanation: Degrees of unsaturation = (2×6 + 2 − 10)/2 = (14 − 10)/2 = 2, consistent with, e.g., one ring plus one double bond, or two double bonds.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: In which functional group is the carbon atom in its HIGHEST oxidation state?
questions.explanation: The carboxyl carbon is bonded to two oxygens (net oxidation state about +3), higher than an aldehyde carbon (+1), an alcohol carbon (about −1), or an alkane CH2 (about −2).

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes an aldehyde from a ketone?
options: In an aldehyde the carbonyl carbon bears at least one hydrogen, whereas in a ketone the carbonyl carbon is bonded to two other carbons.
In an aldehyde the carbonyl carbon is bonded to two carbons; in a ketone it bears a hydrogen.
Aldehydes lack a carbonyl group, while ketones contain one.
Ketones are always more electrophilic at the carbonyl carbon than aldehydes.
questions.explanation: By definition, the aldehyde carbonyl carbon carries at least one H (it sits at a chain end), while a ketone carbonyl carbon is flanked by two carbons.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A molecule contains three stereocenters and possesses no internal symmetry. What is the maximum number of stereoisomers it can have?
questions.explanation: With n stereocenters and no symmetry, the maximum number of stereoisomers is 2^n. For n = 3, that is 2^3 = 8.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Two compounds are enantiomers of each other. In which of the following respects will they DIFFER?
options: Solubility in an ordinary, achiral solvent
The direction in which they rotate plane-polarized light
questions.explanation: Enantiomers share all scalar physical properties in an achiral environment; they differ only in the sign of optical rotation and in their behavior toward other chiral entities.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Tartaric acid can exist as a meso form. Why is the meso stereoisomer optically inactive despite containing two stereocenters?
options: It has an internal mirror plane, making the molecule superimposable on its own mirror image.
It racemizes rapidly at room temperature.
Its two stereocenters bear identical substituents, so R/S cannot be assigned.
questions.explanation: A meso compound has stereocenters but an internal mirror plane; one half's rotation cancels the other, so the molecule is achiral and optically inactive.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which statement about diastereomers is correct?
options: They are stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images of one another and generally have different physical properties.
They are non-superimposable mirror images of each other.
They always have identical physical properties.
They can occur only in molecules with exactly one stereocenter.
questions.explanation: Diastereomers are stereoisomers that are not mirror images (e.g., cis/trans, or differing at one of several stereocenters); unlike enantiomers, they differ in physical properties.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A tetrahedral stereocenter bears –Br, –Cl, –F, and –H. The lowest-priority group (–H) points directly away from the viewer, and tracing from –Br to –Cl to –F follows a counterclockwise path. What is the configuration?
options: Cannot be determined from the information given
questions.explanation: Priority order is Br > Cl > F > H. With the lowest priority pointing away, a counterclockwise 1→2→3 sense corresponds to S.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Consider 1-chloropropene, ClCH=CH–CH3, in the geometry where the chlorine atom and the methyl group lie on the SAME side of the double bond. What is its configuration?
questions.explanation: On C1, Cl outranks H; on C2, CH3 outranks H. When the two higher-priority groups are on the same side, the alkene is designated Z.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A pure chiral compound gives an observed rotation of +2.0° in a 1.0 dm cell at a concentration of 0.10 g/mL. If the concentration is doubled to 0.20 g/mL in the same cell, what observed rotation is expected?
questions.explanation: Observed rotation α = [α] × l × c is directly proportional to concentration and path length. Doubling c (l fixed) doubles the observed rotation to +4.0°.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following molecules is ACHIRAL?
questions.explanation: In 3-chloropentane the chlorine-bearing carbon carries two identical ethyl groups, so it is not a stereocenter; the molecule has a mirror plane and is achiral. The other three each contain a stereocenter.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: The rate of a reaction that proceeds by an SN2 mechanism depends on:
options: The concentrations of both the substrate and the nucleophile
The concentration of the substrate only
The concentration of the nucleophile only
Neither concentration; it is zero order overall
questions.explanation: SN2 is bimolecular: both the substrate and the nucleophile appear in the rate-determining (only) step, so rate = k[substrate][nucleophile].

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: When an optically pure tertiary alkyl halide reacts by an SN1 mechanism, the substitution product is typically:
options: A single enantiomer with complete retention of configuration
A single enantiomer with complete inversion of configuration
A racemic (or nearly racemic) mixture, because the planar carbocation is attacked from either face
questions.explanation: SN1 forms a planar, sp2 carbocation intermediate that the nucleophile can attack from either face with roughly equal probability, producing a racemic (or nearly racemic) mixture.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: An E2 elimination is most favorable when the hydrogen removed and the leaving group are:
options: Separated by at least two carbon atoms
questions.explanation: The concerted E2 transition state requires the C–H and C–LG bonds to be coplanar; the anti-periplanar arrangement is strongly preferred because it aligns the orbitals with minimal strain.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Treating 2-bromobutane with the bulky base potassium tert-butoxide favors which alkene as the major product?
questions.explanation: A sterically hindered base preferentially removes the more accessible proton from the less hindered carbon, giving the less substituted (Hofmann) alkene, 1-butene, as the major product.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: During an SN1/E1 process a secondary carbocation forms directly adjacent to a carbon bearing three methyl groups. What is most likely to happen next?
options: A hydride or methyl shift occurs, generating a more stable tertiary carbocation.
It immediately loses a proton to give the least substituted alkene.
It rearranges to a primary carbocation, which is more stable.
questions.explanation: Carbocations rearrange toward greater stability. A 1,2-hydride or methyl shift converts the secondary cation into the adjacent tertiary cation, which is more stabilized.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: When HBr adds to propene (CH3CH=CH2) in the ABSENCE of peroxides, the major product is:
questions.explanation: Markovnikov addition: the proton adds to the terminal CH2 to give the more stable secondary carbocation at C2, so bromide ends up on C2, yielding 2-bromopropane.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Hydroboration–oxidation of 1-methylcyclohexene installs the –OH group where, and with what stereochemistry?
options: On the more substituted carbon, by anti addition
On the less substituted carbon, by syn addition (anti-Markovnikov)
On the more substituted carbon, by syn addition (Markovnikov)
On the less substituted carbon, by anti addition
questions.explanation: Hydroboration–oxidation adds H and OH in a concerted syn fashion and places OH on the less substituted carbon (anti-Markovnikov), because boron adds to the less hindered position.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Among the halides, which is the BEST leaving group in a nucleophilic substitution reaction?
questions.explanation: Leaving-group ability tracks conjugate-base weakness. Iodide is the weakest base (its charge is spread over a large, polarizable atom), making it the best leaving group.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: In a polar PROTIC solvent such as water or ethanol, which halide ion is the strongest nucleophile?
questions.explanation: In protic solvents, small ions like F⁻ are tightly solvated by hydrogen bonding, which hinders their attack. The large, polarizable, less-solvated iodide is therefore the strongest nucleophile.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Toward nucleophilic addition, aldehydes are generally MORE reactive than ketones primarily because:
options: Aldehydes have a more nucleophilic carbonyl carbon.
Ketones have a partial positive carbonyl carbon while aldehydes do not.
Aldehydes are less sterically hindered, and their carbonyl carbon is more electrophilic because it bears fewer electron-donating alkyl groups.
Ketones cannot undergo nucleophilic addition at all.
questions.explanation: An aldehyde carbonyl carbon has only one alkyl group (vs. two for a ketone), so it is both less shielded sterically and more electrophilic, because alkyl groups donate electron density that stabilizes/deactivates the carbonyl.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Reacting an aldehyde with excess ethanol under acidic conditions gives an acetal. A common synthetic use of acetal formation is to:
options: Protect the carbonyl group from reaction with nucleophiles such as Grignard reagents.
Increase the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon.
Permanently convert the aldehyde into a carboxylic acid.
Introduce a stereocenter that can never be removed.
questions.explanation: Acetals are stable to bases and nucleophiles, so converting a carbonyl to an acetal 'protects' it during a step (e.g., a Grignard reaction elsewhere) and is later reversed by aqueous acid.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Rank the following carboxylic acid derivatives from MOST to LEAST reactive toward nucleophilic acyl substitution.
questions.explanation: Reactivity tracks leaving-group ability and how much the heteroatom donates into the carbonyl: acyl chloride > anhydride > ester > amide (chloride is the best leaving group; the amide nitrogen strongly deactivates the carbonyl).

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: In a base-catalyzed aldol reaction, the nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon is:
options: The carbonyl oxygen of a second molecule
An enolate ion formed by deprotonation of an α-carbon
questions.explanation: Base removes an acidic α-hydrogen to generate a resonance-stabilized enolate; the nucleophilic α-carbon of that enolate then attacks the carbonyl carbon of another molecule.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A Grignard reagent (RMgBr) is added to formaldehyde (H2C=O), followed by aqueous workup. What class of alcohol is produced?
questions.explanation: Formaldehyde has two hydrogens on the carbonyl carbon, so after the R group adds and the alkoxide is protonated, the carbinol carbon bears one R and two H atoms, giving a primary alcohol (RCH2OH).

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which alcohol CANNOT be oxidized to a carbonyl compound by a standard oxidant such as PCC or chromic acid?
questions.explanation: Oxidation of an alcohol to a carbonyl requires removing a hydrogen from the carbinol carbon. A tertiary alcohol has no such hydrogen, so it cannot be oxidized without breaking a C–C bond.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which statement about carbonyl-reducing agents is correct?
options: NaBH4 readily reduces carboxylic acids to primary alcohols.
LiAlH4 is a milder reducing agent than NaBH4.
NaBH4 reduces aldehydes and ketones but is generally too mild for esters and carboxylic acids, whereas LiAlH4 reduces all of them.
Neither reagent can reduce a ketone.
questions.explanation: NaBH4 is a mild hydride source that reduces aldehydes and ketones but generally not esters or carboxylic acids; LiAlH4 is much stronger and reduces esters, acids, amides, and more.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: β-keto acids (a carboxylic acid with a ketone at the β position) lose CO2 readily on gentle heating. Why is this decarboxylation so facile?
options: A cyclic six-membered transition state lets the carbonyl oxygen accept the departing proton, and the resulting enol then tautomerizes to a stable ketone.
CO2 is a poor leaving group, which makes the reaction slow but complete.
The product is a resonance-stabilized carbanion.
questions.explanation: The β-carbonyl oxygen is positioned to accept the acidic proton through a six-membered cyclic transition state, so CO2 leaves and an enol forms, which tautomerizes to the more stable keto product.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Rank the following from MOST to LEAST acidic: ethanol, acetic acid, and phenol.
questions.explanation: Approximate pKa values are acetic acid ≈ 4.8, phenol ≈ 10, ethanol ≈ 16. Lower pKa means stronger acid, so acetic acid > phenol > ethanol.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Carboxylic acids are far more acidic than alcohols primarily because:
options: The O–H bond in a carboxylic acid is weaker for steric reasons.
Carboxylic acids have higher molecular weights than alcohols.
Alcohols cannot lose a proton at all.
The carboxylate conjugate base is stabilized by resonance delocalization of the negative charge over two equivalent oxygen atoms.
questions.explanation: Acid strength is governed by conjugate-base stability. The carboxylate anion spreads its negative charge over two equivalent oxygens by resonance, whereas an alkoxide localizes the charge on one oxygen.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following is the STRONGEST acid?
questions.explanation: An electron-withdrawing halogen stabilizes the carboxylate by induction. Fluorine is the most electronegative, so fluoroacetic acid (pKa ≈ 2.6) is more acidic than chloroacetic (≈ 2.9) and far more than acetic or propanoic acid.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A terminal alkyne (≡C–H, pKa ≈ 25) is considerably more acidic than an alkene (=C–H, pKa ≈ 44). The best explanation is:
options: The alkyne C–H bond is longer than an alkane C–H bond.
The conjugate base places its lone pair in an sp-hybridized orbital, whose greater s-character holds the electrons closer to the nucleus and stabilizes the negative charge.
The alkyne simply has more hydrogen atoms per carbon.
questions.explanation: An sp orbital (50% s-character) holds electrons closer to the positively charged nucleus than an sp2 (33%) orbital, so the acetylide anion is more stable and the terminal alkyne is more acidic.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: You need to deprotonate a terminal alkyne (pKa ≈ 25) essentially completely. Which base is thermodynamically capable of doing so?
options: Sodium amide, NaNH2 (conjugate acid ammonia, pKa ≈ 38)
questions.explanation: A base drives deprotonation to completion only if its conjugate acid is weaker (higher pKa) than the substrate. Ammonia's pKa (38) exceeds 25, so amide fully deprotonates the alkyne; the other bases are too weak.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: According to Hückel's rule, a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated molecule is aromatic when it contains how many π electrons?
options: Any even number of π electrons
questions.explanation: Hückel's rule states that a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated ring is aromatic when it holds 4n+2 π electrons (2, 6, 10, …). Rings with 4n π electrons are antiaromatic.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Which of the following species is ANTIAROMATIC (destabilized) rather than aromatic?
options: Cyclobutadiene (4 π electrons; planar, cyclic, conjugated)
questions.explanation: Cyclobutadiene is cyclic, planar, and conjugated but has 4 π electrons (a 4n count), which makes it antiaromatic and highly unstable. The others all satisfy the 4n+2 rule.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Pyrrole is a very weak base compared with a typical amine. Why?
options: Pyrrole has no lone pair on nitrogen.
The nitrogen's lone pair is part of the aromatic six-π-electron system; protonating the nitrogen would disrupt the aromaticity.
Pyrrole is not a cyclic molecule.
questions.explanation: In pyrrole the nitrogen lone pair is delocalized into the ring to complete the aromatic sextet. Because that lone pair is committed to aromaticity, it is poorly available to bond a proton, so pyrrole is barely basic.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: The cyclopentadienyl anion (C5H5⁻) is unusually stable. This is best explained because:
options: Its 4 π electrons make it antiaromatic.
The negative charge is localized entirely on one carbon.
It is a fully saturated hydrocarbon.
It is aromatic: a planar, cyclic, fully conjugated ring with 6 π electrons (4n+2, n=1).
questions.explanation: Deprotonating cyclopentadiene gives a ring in which the carbanion lone pair joins two double bonds to make a planar, conjugated system of 6 π electrons, satisfying Hückel's rule and making the anion aromatic.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Experimentally, all six carbon–carbon bonds in benzene have the same length, intermediate between a single and a double bond. This is best explained by:
options: Delocalization of the six π electrons over all six carbons (resonance), giving every C–C bond partial double-bond character.
Rapid flipping between two Kekulé structures that are the only real forms present.
Benzene genuinely having alternating single and double bonds of measurably different lengths.
All the carbon atoms being sp3 hybridized.
questions.explanation: The real molecule is a single delocalized π system, not a mixture of localized structures. Every C–C bond experiences the same partial double-bond character, so all six are identical in length.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: 1-Butanol boils at about 118 °C, whereas its constitutional isomer diethyl ether boils at about 35 °C. The large difference is primarily due to:
options: Diethyl ether having a higher molecular weight than 1-butanol.
1-Butanol forming intermolecular hydrogen bonds through its O–H group, which ethers cannot.
Diethyl ether being an ionic compound.
1-Butanol being a gas at room temperature.
questions.explanation: The two isomers have the same molecular weight, but only the alcohol has an O–H that can hydrogen bond between molecules. These stronger intermolecular forces raise its boiling point substantially.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: The Williamson ether synthesis forms an ether from an alkoxide and an alkyl halide. To maximize yield and minimize elimination, the alkyl halide should preferably be:
options: Tertiary, to favor an SN1 pathway
Primary (or methyl), because the reaction proceeds by SN2
questions.explanation: The reaction is an SN2 attack of the alkoxide on the alkyl halide. Primary (or methyl) halides react cleanly, while tertiary halides mostly undergo E2 elimination with the strongly basic alkoxide.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Alcohols are poor SN2 substrates because –OH is a poor leaving group. Converting an alcohol to a tosylate (R–OTs) before substitution helps because:
options: The tosylate (p-toluenesulfonate) is an excellent, weakly basic leaving group, so the C–O bond breaks readily.
The tosylate group is a strong base.
Tosylation makes the attached carbon more nucleophilic.
Tosylation removes the oxygen atom entirely.
questions.explanation: Tosylate is the anion of a strong sulfonic acid, so it is a very weak base and therefore an excellent leaving group; the carbon–oxygen bond then breaks easily during substitution or elimination.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: Trimethylamine, (CH3)3N, is classified as which type of amine?
questions.explanation: Amine classification counts the number of carbon groups bonded to nitrogen. Three carbon groups on a neutral nitrogen makes trimethylamine a tertiary amine.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: When 2-methyl-2-butanol is heated with concentrated sulfuric acid, the major organic product is:
questions.explanation: Acid-catalyzed dehydration proceeds through a tertiary carbocation and follows Zaitsev's rule, giving the more substituted (more stable) alkene, 2-methyl-2-butene, as the major product.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A strong infrared absorption near 1715 cm⁻¹ most directly indicates the presence of which functional group?
options: The O–H of a hydroxyl group
The C=O of a carbonyl group
questions.explanation: Carbonyl (C=O) stretches appear as strong absorptions in the roughly 1700–1750 cm⁻¹ region; about 1715 cm⁻¹ is characteristic of a ketone carbonyl.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: An IR spectrum shows a strong C=O stretch near 1710 cm⁻¹ AND a very broad absorption spanning roughly 2500–3300 cm⁻¹. Which functional group best fits this pattern?
questions.explanation: A carboxylic acid shows both a carbonyl stretch near 1710 cm⁻¹ and a very broad O–H stretch (about 2500–3300 cm⁻¹) from strong hydrogen bonding. An alcohol lacks the C=O, and an aldehyde lacks the broad acidic O–H.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: How many chemically distinct hydrogen environments (¹H NMR signals) does ethanol, CH3CH2OH, produce?
questions.explanation: Ethanol has three sets of chemically distinct hydrogens: the CH3 protons, the CH2 protons, and the O–H proton, giving three ¹H NMR signals.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: In the ¹H NMR spectrum of an isolated ethyl group (–CH2–CH3), the CH3 protons appear as a triplet. By the n+1 rule, this splitting arises because the CH3 protons are coupled to:
options: The three equivalent protons of the CH3 group itself
The two equivalent protons of the adjacent CH2 group
A single proton located three bonds away
The carbon-13 nucleus of the same carbon
questions.explanation: The n+1 rule counts equivalent neighboring protons. The CH3 has two adjacent CH2 protons (n = 2), so it is split into n+1 = 3 lines (a triplet). Equivalent protons on the same carbon do not split each other.

questions.subject: Organic Chemistry
questions.stem: A ¹H NMR signal appearing far downfield near 9.7 ppm is most characteristic of which proton?
options: A methyl proton on an sp3 carbon
